snappy: A federal program that forces drugmakers to sell heavily discounted medicines to hospitals and clinics serving low-income patients — controversial because of how the savings get used.
detail: Created in 1992, 340B requires manufacturers to give qualifying safety-net providers steep discounts (often 25-50% off list price) on outpatient drugs. The program has ballooned in size and become a flashpoint: pharma argues hospitals pocket the savings instead of passing them to patients, while hospitals say it funds critical care. You'll see 340B fights surface in earnings calls, congressional testimony, and lawsuits between manufacturers and HRSA.

snappy: An FDA fast-track that lets a drug reach patients based on early evidence — but requires the company to confirm benefit in follow-up trials, or risk getting yanked.
detail: Accelerated approval relies on a surrogate measure — like tumor shrinkage instead of survival — to grant approval years earlier than full evidence would allow. The pathway has been controversial: Aduhelm (aducanumab) for Alzheimer's was accelerated, then commercially failed. Companies must run confirmatory trials post-approval, and the FDA can withdraw the drug if benefit doesn't hold. You'll hear "accel approval" on every oncology earnings call and at ASCO when companies discuss launch timing.

snappy: A class of blood-pressure drugs that blocks an enzyme the body uses to constrict blood vessels — first-line for hypertension, heart failure, and protecting the kidneys.
detail: ACE inhibitors — lisinopril, enalapril, ramipril — and the closely related angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs, like Diovan/valsartan) are workhorse cardiovascular drugs, all generic and prescribed by the millions. The 2018 valsartan recalls over nitrosamine contamination became one of pharma's biggest manufacturing crises. You'll hear ACE/ARB on cardio earnings calls as the durable generic baseline newer mechanisms have to compete with, and in FDA Form 483 discussions when API supply-chain quality surfaces.

snappy: A clinical trial that lets researchers change course mid-study — adjusting dose, dropping a treatment arm, or expanding enrollment — based on what early data shows.
detail: Adaptive designs use pre-specified rules so changes don't compromise statistical integrity. Common adaptations include sample-size re-estimation, response-adaptive randomization, and seamless Phase 2/3 transitions. The FDA published guidance encouraging the approach, and COVID-era trials like RECOVERY popularized it. You'll hear adaptive design pitched at every R&D day as a way to cut development time and cost, and at ASCO when trials use early-stage signals to decide which combinations to push forward.

snappy: Think of it as a guided missile for cancer: an antibody that hunts down tumor cells, fused to a chemo drug that destroys them on contact.
detail: ADCs deliver toxic chemotherapy directly to cancer cells while sparing healthy ones, which means stronger results with fewer side effects than traditional chemo. They're a fast-growing class across breast, bladder, and lung cancers — well-known examples include Kadcyla (trastuzumab emtansine) and Enhertu.

snappy: Cancer that starts in cells lining your organs and making mucus or fluids — the most common form of lung, colon, prostate, and pancreatic cancer.
detail: Most cancers diagnosed in the lung, colon, prostate, and pancreas are adenocarcinomas — meaning they originate in glandular tissue rather than skin-like or supporting cells. The distinction matters because different cancer types respond to different treatments. You'll see the term in pathology reports, oncology trial enrollment criteria, and any commercial discussion of which patient population a new drug is targeting.

snappy: A condition marked by trouble focusing, controlling impulses, and sitting still — most often diagnosed in childhood, but increasingly recognized and treated in adults too.
detail: ADHD treatment leans heavily on stimulants like Adderall (mixed amphetamine salts) and Ritalin (methylphenidate), supplemented by non-stimulants like Strattera (atomoxetine). Adult diagnosis has surged, driving multi-year US shortages of generic Adderall that became front-page news in 2023. You'll hear ADHD pipelines pitched at investor days as a high-growth segment, and DEA quotas on stimulant manufacturing surface regularly in earnings calls and supply commentary.

snappy: The follow-up treatment after surgery or radiation, designed to mop up cancer cells you can't see and stop the disease coming back.
detail: Adjuvant therapy — usually chemo, hormone therapy, or immunotherapy — is what oncologists use to lower the chance of recurrence after the visible tumor has been removed. You'll hear it constantly in breast, colon, and lung cancer trial readouts, often framed around the question "did adjuvant treatment improve survival?"

snappy: The four-step journey every drug takes through your body — getting in, getting around, getting broken down, and getting out.
detail: ADME is the framework drug developers use to predict how a drug behaves once it enters the body. Each step affects how much drug reaches the target, how long it lasts, and what side effects emerge. You'll hear ADME on biotech earnings calls when companies discuss whether a candidate has good "drug-like properties," and in early-stage trial design where these properties first get measured.

snappy: Any unwanted or harmful effect a patient experiences during a clinical trial — whether or not the drug actually caused it.
detail: Adverse events are tracked exhaustively in trials because regulators need to know what users might experience. The term covers everything from a mild headache to a serious hospitalization — the more severe ones get reclassified as Serious Adverse Events (SAEs). You'll hear AEs come up in trial readouts ("the safety profile was consistent with…") and in FDA review letters that decide whether a drug gets approved.

snappy: A drug that flips a switch to activate a receptor in your body — telling cells to do something, like a thermostat dialing up the heat.
detail: Agonists mimic natural signaling molecules — when one binds to a receptor, the receptor activates as if its natural partner had arrived. Many medications work this way: opioids agonize pain receptors; asthma inhalers like albuterol agonize beta-2 receptors to relax airways. You'll hear the term in pharmacology basics and any discussion of how a new drug actually does its job in the body.

snappy: A key signaling protein that pushes cells to grow and survive — and is often hijacked by cancer to fuel its own growth.
detail: AKT (also called Protein Kinase B) sits at the center of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway, one of the most commonly disrupted signaling networks in cancer. When AKT is overactive, cells refuse to die and keep multiplying — so drugs that block it have been a long-running area of cancer research. You'll hear AKT in oncology trial designs, pipeline discussions, and commercial pitches involving "pathway inhibitors."

snappy: A gene mutation that drives about 5% of lung cancers — and the target of some of oncology's biggest precision-medicine wins.
detail: ALK gene fusions cause uncontrolled cell growth in a subset of non-small-cell lung cancer patients. The story is a precision-medicine showcase: identify the mutation, prescribe a targeted drug like Xalkori (crizotinib) or Alecensa (alectinib), and patients live dramatically longer than they would on chemo. You'll hear ALK in lung cancer trial readouts and at ASCO whenever new generations of inhibitors are presented.

snappy: A fast-growing blood cancer that starts in immature lymphocytes — the most common cancer in children, though it also affects adults.
detail: ALL develops when lymphoid cells in the bone marrow turn cancerous and crowd out healthy blood cells. Treatment is intensive — combination chemo, sometimes a bone marrow transplant, increasingly CAR-T cell therapy in relapsed cases. You'll hear ALL discussed in pediatric oncology contexts and in any commercial story around CAR-T (Kymriah was approved for pediatric and young-adult ALL in 2017).

snappy: A progressive brain disease — the most common form of dementia — where abnormal protein clumps build up and slowly destroy memory, thinking, and the ability to live independently.
detail: Alzheimer's affects roughly 6 million Americans. Symptomatic drugs like donepezil and memantine offer modest benefit; the new disease-modifying anti-amyloid antibodies — Leqembi (lecanemab) and Kisunla (donanemab) — slow decline by 25-35% but require infusions, MRI monitoring for brain swelling, and have driven uneven Medicare coverage. You'll hear Alzheimer's at every Eli Lilly and Biogen-Eisai earnings call, and at CTAD when readouts on next-generation drugs targeting tau and earlier intervention shape investor expectations.

snappy: A fast-moving blood cancer that starts in the bone marrow and demands urgent treatment — primarily affects older adults but can hit at any age.
detail: AML develops when myeloid cells — the precursors to red cells, platelets, and most white cells — become cancerous and overrun the bone marrow. Treatment is aggressive (high-dose chemo, often bone marrow transplant) and increasingly genotype-driven, with FLT3 and IDH inhibitors targeting specific mutations. You'll hear AML in oncology pipeline discussions, ASCO/ASH conference coverage, and around companies developing targeted leukemia therapies.

snappy: The shortcut a generic drug company files instead of a full new-drug application — proving its copy is bioequivalent to the original branded drug.
detail: ANDAs let generic manufacturers skip duplicating safety and efficacy trials, since the original drug already proved them — they only need to show their version delivers the same active ingredient at the same rate. After the brand's patents expire, multiple generics typically file ANDAs to compete on price. You'll see ANDA filings drive earnings volatility for any company with a major drug nearing patent cliff, and "first-to-file" generics earn 180-day exclusivity.

snappy: The process where the body builds new blood vessels — which tumors hijack to feed their own growth.
detail: Tumors trick surrounding tissue into growing new blood vessels that supply oxygen and nutrients, letting them expand beyond what local blood supply can sustain. Drugs that block this process — including Avastin (bevacizumab) — are a major class in oncology. You'll hear angiogenesis come up in solid-tumor trial designs and in commercial discussions of how "anti-angiogenics" fit alongside immunotherapy and chemo.

snappy: A drug that blocks a receptor — like sliding tape over a light switch so it can't be turned on.
detail: Antagonists work by occupying a receptor site without activating it, preventing the body's natural signaling molecule from binding. Beta blockers like propranolol are antagonists at beta-adrenergic receptors, slowing the heart. Antihistamines block histamine receptors. You'll hear the term in pharmacology basics, in any drug-development discussion of mechanism, and on earnings calls when companies position their drug against a competitor's.

snappy: A drug that kills or stops the growth of bacteria — the foundation of modern medicine since penicillin, and increasingly under threat as bacteria evolve resistance.
detail: Antibiotic classes range from penicillins and cephalosporins to fluoroquinolones, macrolides, and last-resort drugs like vancomycin and carbapenems. Despite enormous public-health value, antibiotics earn poor commercial returns — most are short-course generics — and the pipeline has thinned. You'll hear antibiotics framed on policy panels and at JPMorgan as a market-failure problem, and in PASTEUR Act discussions about subscription-style government payment models to revive the pipeline.

snappy: A blood thinner — a drug that slows the clotting cascade so dangerous clots don't form in your veins, lungs, or brain.
detail: The class spans heparin and warfarin (cheap, requires monitoring) and the newer direct oral anticoagulants — Eliquis (apixaban), Xarelto (rivaroxaban), Pradaxa (dabigatran) — that don't require blood-test monitoring. DOACs have largely displaced warfarin in atrial fibrillation and venous thromboembolism. You'll hear anticoagulants in every BMS-Pfizer commentary on Eliquis franchise revenue, in IRA negotiation news (Eliquis is on the first Medicare list), and in stroke-prevention policy discussions.

snappy: What happens when bacteria, viruses, or fungi evolve to survive the drugs designed to kill them — turning routine infections into life-threatening ones.
detail: AMR is responsible for roughly 1.3 million deaths globally each year and rising, driven by overuse of antibiotics in human medicine and livestock. The CDC tracks priority pathogens like MRSA, drug-resistant gonorrhea, and CRE; the WHO calls AMR one of the top global health threats. You'll hear AMR at WHO assemblies, in CDC press releases, and at pharma policy panels arguing for new economic models — including the proposed PASTEUR Act — to fund antibiotic R&D that markets won't.

snappy: A drug that targets viruses — typically by blocking the proteins they need to enter cells, copy themselves, or assemble new particles — without harming healthy human cells.
detail: Antiviral classes span HIV (integrase inhibitors, NRTIs), hepatitis C (cures like Harvoni/ledipasvir-sofosbuvir), influenza (Tamiflu/oseltamivir), herpes (acyclovir), and COVID (Paxlovid/nirmatrelvir-ritonavir). Unlike antibiotics, several blockbuster antiviral franchises remain — Gilead's HIV portfolio funded its rise. You'll hear antivirals at every Gilead, Pfizer, and Merck earnings call when COVID seasonality and HIV pipelines come up, and in WHO press communications about influenza pandemic readiness.

snappy: Excessive, persistent worry that interferes with daily life — covering generalized anxiety, panic, social phobia, and PTSD as a related family of conditions.
detail: Anxiety disorders are the most common mental health condition in the US, affecting roughly 19% of adults. Treatment combines therapy with SSRIs, SNRIs, or short-term benzodiazepines like Xanax (alprazolam), though benzo dependence is a known risk. Newer entrants target GABA pathways without sedation. You'll hear anxiety markets framed at JPMorgan as massive but commoditized, and on investor days when companies pitch novel mechanisms designed to avoid the dependence problem of older sedatives.

snappy: A tumor-suppressor gene whose job is to keep cells from dividing out of control — and its breakdown is the first step toward most colorectal cancers.
detail: APC mutations are among the earliest genetic changes in colorectal cancer development — they're found in roughly 80% of sporadic cases and in nearly all patients with familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP), an inherited cancer syndrome. You'll hear APC in colorectal cancer screening discussions, hereditary-cancer-syndrome contexts, and in any pipeline conversation involving Wnt-pathway-targeted therapies.

snappy: The actual chemical inside a pill or injection that does the work — everything else (fillers, coatings, capsule) is just delivery.
detail: An API is what's pharmacologically active in a finished drug; the inactive components — called excipients — give it shape, stability, and a delivery mechanism. APIs are typically manufactured at specialized facilities, often in India or China, before being formulated into the final product. You'll hear API in supply-chain discussions (especially around shortages), at drug-pricing debates, and on earnings calls when companies discuss vertical integration.

snappy: Programmed cell death — the body's built-in self-destruct mechanism for damaged or unwanted cells. Cancer cells routinely figure out how to switch it off.
detail: Apoptosis is how the body removes cells that are no longer needed or are too damaged to function — about a million cells per second, in normal physiology. Cancer cells acquire mutations that disable apoptosis, allowing them to keep dividing instead of self-destructing. You'll hear apoptosis come up in cancer-mechanism discussions and in any drug-development pitch around restoring cell death (e.g., BH3 mimetics, BCL-2 inhibitors).

snappy: A laboratory test that measures something — usually whether a drug works, how much of it is present, or what it does to cells.
detail: Assays are the workhorses of drug development: high-throughput screens to find compounds that hit a target, ELISAs to detect proteins in blood, cell-based assays to measure tumor killing. Diagnostic companies sell assays as products in their own right. You'll hear the term across early-stage research papers, biotech investor decks describing platforms, and any FDA discussion of analytical validation.

snappy: A chronic condition where the airways tighten, swell, and fill with mucus — causing wheezing, coughing, and shortness of breath that ranges from mild to life-threatening.
detail: Asthma treatment escalates from rescue inhalers (albuterol) to maintenance inhaled corticosteroids and combination LABA/ICS inhalers like Advair, then to biologics for severe disease — Dupixent (dupilumab), Xolair (omalizumab), Nucala (mepolizumab), Tezspire (tezepelumab). The biologic shift has split the market between cheap generic inhalers and high-priced injectables. You'll hear asthma on every GSK, Sanofi-Regeneron, and AstraZeneca earnings call, and at ATS when biomarker-defined patient subsets reshape which biologic wins for whom.

snappy: A brain tumor that arises from star-shaped support cells called astrocytes — the family includes glioblastoma, the most aggressive.
detail: Astrocytomas range from slow-growing low-grade tumors to glioblastoma multiforme — one of the deadliest cancers, with median survival measured in months despite surgery, radiation, and chemo. The category is graded I to IV by aggressiveness. You'll hear astrocytoma in neuro-oncology contexts, brain-cancer trial readouts, and around clinical-trial sponsors developing therapies for the underserved glioblastoma population.

snappy: A gene that helps cells fix damaged DNA — when it's broken, cells accumulate mutations and cancer risk goes up.
detail: ATM is a key DNA-damage response gene; people who inherit mutations in both copies develop ataxia telangiectasia, a rare disorder with sharply elevated cancer risk. ATM mutations also turn up in breast, prostate, and pancreatic cancers, where they may flag tumors that respond to PARP inhibitors. You'll hear ATM in precision-oncology trial designs and any commercial discussion of biomarker-driven drug selection.

snappy: An irregular, often fast heartbeat caused by chaotic electrical signals in the upper chambers — the most common heart-rhythm problem and a leading cause of stroke.
detail: Afib treatment has two arms: rhythm/rate control (drugs like amiodarone or procedures like ablation) and stroke prevention with anticoagulants. The shift from warfarin to direct oral anticoagulants — Eliquis (apixaban), Xarelto (rivaroxaban) — is one of the biggest commercial stories of the last decade. You'll hear afib on every BMS and Pfizer earnings call given Eliquis's status as one of pharma's largest drugs, and at HRS when ablation device makers report procedure volumes.

snappy: When the body's immune system mistakes its own healthy cells for invaders and attacks them — sometimes triggered by cancer immunotherapy.
detail: Autoimmune diseases — rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, type 1 diabetes, multiple sclerosis — affect roughly 5% of the population and are a major commercial market for biologic drugs like Humira (adalimumab) and Stelara (ustekinumab). Cancer immunotherapy can also trigger autoimmune-like side effects called immune-related adverse events. You'll hear autoimmune in immunology pipeline discussions, biologic earnings calls, and immunotherapy trial readouts.

snappy: The most common skin cancer — slow-growing, rarely spreads to other organs, but can quietly cause real damage to the skin and underlying tissue if left alone.
detail: BCC arises from the deepest layer of the skin's outer surface, usually on sun-exposed areas like the face, ears, and neck. Treatment is typically surgical removal, with high cure rates. For advanced or rare metastatic cases, hedgehog-pathway inhibitors like Erivedge (vismodegib) are options. You'll hear BCC in dermatology drug development and in commercial discussions of skin-cancer immunotherapy and topical agents.

snappy: A protein that helps cells avoid death — when it's overactive, cancer cells refuse to die, which is how many blood cancers stay alive.
detail: BCL-2 was the first cancer gene discovered to work by blocking apoptosis (programmed cell death) rather than by driving growth. It's overexpressed in CLL, follicular lymphoma, and AML, and Venclexta (venetoclax) is a BCL-2 inhibitor that has reshaped treatment in those settings. You'll hear BCL-2 at hematology conferences (ASH especially) and in any pipeline discussion involving "apoptosis-targeted" oncology.

snappy: A fused gene that drives chronic myeloid leukemia — and the target of one of the first and most successful precision-medicine drugs ever made.
detail: When parts of chromosomes 9 and 22 swap places, they form the abnormal BCR-ABL gene that produces a constantly active growth signal in white blood cells, causing CML. The story is legendary in oncology: Gleevec (imatinib), launched in 2001, transformed CML from a fatal disease into a manageable chronic condition. You'll hear BCR-ABL in any drug-development pitch invoking targeted therapy as a paradigm.

snappy: How much of a drug actually makes it into your bloodstream after you take it — the rest is broken down or never absorbed.
detail: When you swallow a pill, only a fraction reaches the circulation; the rest is destroyed by stomach acid, liver enzymes, or never absorbed by the gut. IV drugs are 100% bioavailable by definition; pills can range from 5% to 95% depending on chemistry. You'll hear the term in early drug development, formulation discussions, and at FDA reviews of generic-drug equivalence.

snappy: A drug grown from living cells rather than synthesized in a chemistry lab — antibodies, proteins, vaccines, and gene therapies all fall under the biologic umbrella.
detail: Biologics are larger and more complex than small-molecule pills, must be injected or infused, and cost far more to manufacture — driving high prices and the rise of biosimilars after patents expire. Examples span Humira (adalimumab) for autoimmune disease, Keytruda (pembrolizumab) for cancer, and the COVID mRNA vaccines. You'll hear biologic versus small-molecule debates everywhere in pharma — at JPMorgan when companies pitch their pipeline mix, and on earnings calls when biosimilar erosion forecasts hit.

snappy: A measurable biological signal — like a fingerprint your body leaves behind — that tells doctors what's happening inside you.
detail: Biomarkers can be molecules in your blood (such as PSA for prostate cancer), genetic mutations (such as HER2 in breast cancer), or imaging signals. Pharma uses them to diagnose disease, predict which patients will respond to a drug, and track whether a treatment is working — they're the foundation of "precision medicine."

snappy: Taking a small piece of tissue from your body to examine under a microscope — usually to confirm whether something is cancer.
detail: Biopsies range from a needle aspirate (a few cells from a lymph node) to a surgical excision (a whole lump removed). The sample is processed by a pathologist who diagnoses the disease and increasingly identifies genetic mutations that guide treatment selection. You'll hear biopsy in cancer trial design ("tumor biopsy required"), liquid-biopsy startup discussions, and any commercial conversation about companion diagnostics.

snappy: A near-identical copy of an existing biologic drug — like a generic, but more complicated to make because biologics are produced by living cells.
detail: Unlike small-molecule generics, biosimilars can't be exactly identical to their reference biologic — biology doesn't allow it — so the FDA approves them based on showing no clinically meaningful differences. They drive down prices when biologics like Remicade (infliximab) and Humira (adalimumab) lose patent protection. You'll hear biosimilar in payer discussions about cost savings and at investor briefings on biologic patent cliffs.

snappy: An engineered antibody with two grabbers instead of one — designed to grab a cancer cell with one hand and an immune cell with the other, forcing them together.
detail: Bispecifics — also called BiTEs (Bispecific T-cell Engagers) — physically yoke a tumor cell to a T-cell so the immune system kills it on contact. Approved examples include Blincyto (blinatumomab) for leukemia and Tecvayli (teclistamab) for multiple myeloma. The class has expanded fast across blood and solid cancers, and is one of the most-watched modalities at ASCO and in oncology investor decks.

snappy: The paperwork a company files with the FDA to win approval to sell a biologic drug — anything from a vaccine to an antibody to a cell therapy.
detail: BLAs go to the FDA's biologics center (CBER) or drug center (CDER) depending on the product, and run on the PDUFA review clock just like small-molecule applications. Recent high-profile BLA approvals include bluebird's gene therapies for sickle cell and several GLP-1 biologics. You'll hear "BLA filing" celebrated in press releases and earnings calls — it marks the moment a company has handed its full dossier to regulators and is awaiting the verdict.

snappy: The FDA's strongest safety alert — a warning printed in a literal black-bordered box on a drug's label when the risks are serious enough to demand prescriber attention.
detail: Black Box Warnings are added when post-market surveillance or clinical trials reveal a risk of serious harm — well-known examples include certain antidepressants flagged for suicide risk in young patients, and some immunotherapies for severe autoimmune side effects. The warning doesn't pull a drug from the market; it requires prescribers to weigh the risk explicitly. You'll see the term in FDA press releases, legal proceedings, and any commercial discussion of a drug's market trajectory after the warning lands.

snappy: Cancer that starts in the lining of the bladder — most often in long-time smokers — and frequently recurs even after early-stage treatment.
detail: Bladder cancer is overwhelmingly urothelial carcinoma, with FGFR3 and TP53 mutations driving subsets. Treatment ranges from intravesical BCG for early-stage disease to systemic therapy for advanced cases — Keytruda (pembrolizumab), Padcev (enfortumab vedotin), and the FGFR inhibitor Balversa (erdafitinib) are recent additions. You'll hear bladder cancer at ASCO in commercial discussions of immunotherapy and ADC market expansion.

snappy: Hiding from patients, doctors, or both whether a person is getting the real drug or a placebo — so expectations don't bias the results.
detail: Trials can be single-blind (only patients don't know) or double-blind (neither patients nor doctors know); placebo bias is real and measurable, so blinding is essential to clean results. The unblinded readout is what triggers stock-moving headlines. You'll hear blinding in trial-design discussions, FDA review documents, and at investor conferences when companies preview Phase 3 data they expect to release.

snappy: The body's bouncer for the brain — a tight wall of cells that keeps most chemicals (including most drugs) from crossing from the blood to the brain.
detail: The BBB protects the brain from toxins but also keeps out most therapeutic drugs, making CNS conditions especially hard to treat. Crossing the BBB is a central engineering problem in neurology drug development — solved with small-molecule design, antibody shuttles, or ultrasound. You'll hear BBB in pipeline discussions for Alzheimer's, brain cancer, and any neuro-immunotherapy conversation, often as the reason something failed.

snappy: A gene that, when mutated, drives uncontrolled cell growth — found in about half of all melanomas, plus some lung and colon cancers.
detail: The BRAF V600E mutation makes the protein constantly "on," pushing cells to proliferate. Tafinlar (dabrafenib) plus Mekinist (trametinib) is the standard combo for BRAF-mutant melanoma — together they shrink tumors dramatically, though resistance often develops. You'll hear BRAF in melanoma trial readouts, precision-oncology discussions, and in any company commentary on tumor-agnostic mutation testing.

snappy: Cancer that has spread from somewhere else in the body — usually lung, breast, or melanoma — into the brain.
detail: Once cancer reaches the brain, treatment options narrow because most drugs can't cross the blood-brain barrier; whole-brain radiation, stereotactic radiosurgery, and a small group of brain-penetrant drugs (Tagrisso, Alecensa, Tukysa) become the toolkit. You'll hear brain metastases in oncology trial-enrollment criteria (often excluded) and in any drug pitch claiming "CNS activity" — a key commercial differentiator.

snappy: Two genes that normally help cells repair damaged DNA — when inherited as broken copies, they sharply raise the lifetime risk of breast and ovarian cancer.
detail: BRCA mutations affect roughly 1 in 400 people; carriers face up to 70% lifetime breast cancer risk and elevated risk for ovarian, prostate, and pancreatic cancers. The genes are also a treatment target — PARP inhibitors like Lynparza (olaparib) work especially well on BRCA-mutated tumors. You'll hear BRCA in genetic-testing conversations, oncology trial enrollment criteria, and at any company developing PARP-class therapies.

snappy: An FDA badge granted to drugs targeting serious conditions when early evidence suggests they're substantially better than existing options — bringing intensive agency support along the way.
detail: Created in 2012, Breakthrough Therapy is one of four FDA expedited programs and includes more frequent FDA meetings, intensive guidance on trial design, and eligibility for rolling review. Roughly half of recent oncology approvals carried the designation. You'll see "Breakthrough" stamped on press releases the day it's granted — pharma stocks often pop on the news, and analysts track the running tally of breakthrough drugs at every JPMorgan.

snappy: The most common cancer in women — classified by which receptors are present (estrogen, progesterone, HER2), because the receptors decide treatment.
detail: Breast cancer subtypes — ER+, PR+, HER2+, triple-negative — drive entirely different treatment paths: hormone therapies for ER+, HER2-targeted drugs like Herceptin (trastuzumab) and ADCs like Enhertu for HER2+, and immunotherapy plus chemo for triple-negative. You'll hear breast cancer almost everywhere in oncology — at every major conference, in every pharma pipeline, and in any commercial discussion of women's-health markets.

snappy: A signaling protein that helps cancerous B-cells survive and multiply — and the target of a major class of leukemia and lymphoma drugs.
detail: BTK is essential for B-cell development; in CLL and certain lymphomas, blocking it stops the cancer's growth signals. Imbruvica (ibrutinib) and Calquence (acalabrutinib) are the leading BTK inhibitors and major commercial products. You'll hear BTK at ASH, in CLL trial readouts, and in pipeline discussion of next-generation "covalent" or "non-covalent" inhibitors and their resistance profiles.

snappy: A very fast-growing blood cancer of B-cells — one of the most aggressive cancers known, but often curable with intensive chemo when caught early.
detail: Burkitt lymphoma is driven by a translocation that activates the MYC gene — the cancer cells double in less than a day, making it one of the fastest-growing tumors. Treatment is intensive, hospital-based combination chemotherapy, with cure rates exceeding 80% in younger patients. You'll hear Burkitt in hematology pipeline discussions and at ASH, often referenced as a benchmark for aggressive lymphoma biology.

snappy: A treatment where doctors take your own immune cells, reprogram them in a lab to recognize cancer, and infuse them back to attack the disease.
detail: CAR-T therapies engineer T-cells with a synthetic receptor that targets a specific cancer marker (typically CD19 on B-cell cancers). Approved CAR-Ts include Kymriah (tisagenlecleucel) and Yescarta (axicabtagene ciloleucel), with cure-level responses in some leukemias and lymphomas. The therapies are expensive ($400-500k) and require specialty centers. You'll hear CAR-T at ASH, in commercial discussions of cell-therapy market access, and around new solid-tumor expansion attempts.

snappy: Cancer that starts in the cells lining your skin, organs, and tissues — covers most of the cancers you've heard of.
detail: Carcinomas — including adenocarcinomas, squamous cell carcinomas, and basal cell carcinomas — account for around 80-90% of all cancer diagnoses. The category is broad: lung, breast, prostate, colon, skin, head-and-neck cancers all fall here. You'll see carcinoma in almost every pathology report and oncology trial-enrollment criterion, distinguishing solid epithelial tumors from blood cancers and sarcomas.

snappy: Two proteins that act as the cell's accelerator pedal — when overactive in cancer, drugs that block them slam the brakes on tumor growth.
detail: CDK4/6 inhibitors — Ibrance (palbociclib), Kisqali (ribociclib), and Verzenio (abemaciclib) — have transformed treatment for ER-positive, HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer, doubling progression-free survival when combined with hormone therapy. The class is one of the biggest oncology launches of the past decade. You'll hear CDK4/6 in breast-cancer trial readouts, ASCO commercial discussions, and any pipeline conversation about expansion into adjuvant breast or other tumor types.

snappy: A diagnostic test paired with a specific drug — used to find the patients whose biology makes the drug likely to actually work for them.
detail: The FDA approves many targeted therapies — especially in oncology — only alongside an approved diagnostic test that confirms the patient has the specific mutation or protein the drug targets. Herceptin (trastuzumab) for HER2-positive breast cancer is the textbook example. You'll hear CDx in approval announcements and commercial discussions, where access to testing often matters as much as access to the drug itself.

snappy: A class of cancer drugs that releases the immune system's brakes — letting your own T-cells recognize and attack tumors that have been hiding.
detail: Checkpoint inhibitors block proteins like PD-1, PD-L1, and CTLA-4 that normally restrain the immune system. By taking the brakes off, drugs like Keytruda (pembrolizumab), Opdivo (nivolumab), and Yervoy (ipilimumab) have turned metastatic melanoma and lung cancer from often-fatal to often-manageable. You'll hear checkpoint inhibitor everywhere in oncology — at every conference, in every pharma pipeline, and in any commercial discussion of immuno-oncology combinations.

snappy: Drug treatment that kills fast-growing cells — devastating for cancer cells, but also tough on hair, gut lining, and bone marrow.
detail: "Chemo" historically meant cytotoxic drugs that target rapidly dividing cells; the side effects (hair loss, nausea, immune suppression) come from the same mechanism damaging healthy cells that also divide quickly. Modern oncology blends chemo with targeted therapies and immunotherapies. You'll still hear chemotherapy at every cancer-treatment discussion, in trial readouts as a comparator arm, and in patient experience around tolerability.

snappy: A rare cancer of the bile ducts — the small tubes that carry digestive juices from the liver to the small intestine.
detail: Cholangiocarcinoma is uncommon but increasingly the focus of precision oncology because subsets carry IDH1/2 mutations or FGFR2 fusions, both of which now have approved targeted therapies (Tibsovo, Truseltiq, Pemazyre). You'll hear cholangiocarcinoma in rare-cancer trial readouts and in commercial discussions of how precision medicine creates niche-but-defensible markets in difficult-to-treat tumors.

snappy: A slow-growing blood cancer of B-cells, usually in older adults — many patients live for years with monitoring before any treatment is needed.
detail: CLL is the most common leukemia in adults and often causes few symptoms early on, with a "watch and wait" approach for low-risk patients. When treatment becomes necessary, BTK inhibitors like Imbruvica (ibrutinib) and BCL-2 inhibitors like Venclexta (venetoclax) have largely replaced chemotherapy. You'll hear CLL constantly at ASH and in commercial pipelines, often as a stepping stone toward other B-cell-cancer indications.

snappy: A blood cancer driven by a single gene fusion — and the historic proof that targeted drugs can turn cancer into a manageable chronic disease.
detail: CML is caused by the BCR-ABL fusion gene (the "Philadelphia chromosome"), and Gleevec (imatinib) was the first drug to specifically inhibit it — turning a once-fatal cancer into a long-term survivable condition with a daily pill. Newer-generation TKIs like Tasigna (nilotinib), Sprycel (dasatinib), and Scemblix (asciminib) handle resistance. You'll hear CML referenced as the textbook precision-medicine success story.

snappy: A research study that tests whether a new drug, device, or treatment is safe and works — done in carefully controlled groups of volunteer patients.
detail: Clinical trials run in phases: Phase 1 tests safety in a small group, Phase 2 tests effectiveness, Phase 3 confirms benefit in large populations to support approval, and Phase 4 monitors after approval. The whole drug-development industry runs on these readouts — they drive stock prices, deal-making, and regulatory decisions. You'll hear "clinical trial" everywhere in pharma; understanding the phase usually tells you the stakes.

snappy: The detailed package describing how a drug is actually made, tested, and kept consistent — required for FDA approval and ongoing oversight.
detail: CMC documents cover every step from raw materials to final product: synthesis routes, formulation, testing methods, stability data, manufacturing-facility audits. Weak CMC packages are a common reason FDA delays approval even when efficacy data is strong. You'll hear CMC in late-stage drug development discussions and at FDA Complete Response Letters that cite manufacturing issues rather than clinical concerns.

snappy: An unbroken refrigeration network from factory to patient — required for biologics, vaccines, and cell therapies that lose potency the moment they warm up.
detail: Cold chain logistics range from standard 2-8°C refrigeration for most biologics to ultra-cold -70°C handling for the original Pfizer COVID vaccine and cell therapies like Kymriah (tisagenlecleucel). A single excursion outside spec can force a shipment to be discarded. You'll hear cold chain framed as a barrier in commercial discussions of biologic launches in emerging markets, and as a competitive moat for companies that have built reliable logistics partnerships.

snappy: Cancer of the colon or rectum — the third most common cancer globally, and one of the most preventable through screening colonoscopies.
detail: CRC develops over years from precancerous polyps that screening can catch. Treatment depends on stage and molecular profile: surgery for early disease; chemo plus drugs like Erbitux (cetuximab) for KRAS-wild-type metastatic disease; immunotherapy for the small subset with high microsatellite instability. You'll hear CRC at GI-focused oncology meetings and in any commercial discussion of biomarker-guided treatment selection.

snappy: The flat fee you pay at the pharmacy counter when you fill a prescription — separate from your premium and your deductible.
detail: Copays are set by your insurance plan and vary by formulary tier — generics might cost $5, preferred brands $30, specialty drugs hundreds or thousands. Pharma often offers "copay cards" to cover the patient's share for branded drugs, a practice insurers and PBMs argue inflates overall spend. You'll hear copay strategy debated on launch-readiness calls and in commercial discussions of how patients actually access a newly approved drug.

snappy: Long-term lung damage, mostly from smoking, that makes breathing progressively harder and can't be reversed — only slowed and managed with the right treatment.
detail: COPD covers chronic bronchitis and emphysema and affects roughly 16 million diagnosed Americans, with millions more undiagnosed. Treatment relies on inhaled bronchodilators (LABA, LAMA) and corticosteroids — Trelegy (fluticasone-umeclidinium-vilanterol) is the leading triple-therapy inhaler. The first IL-targeting biologic, Dupixent (dupilumab), was approved for COPD in 2024, opening a new chapter. You'll hear COPD on GSK, AstraZeneca, and Sanofi-Regeneron earnings calls as a market shifting from generic inhalers to biologics.

snappy: A precision gene-editing tool — molecular scissors that can cut, replace, or silence specific stretches of DNA — and the foundation of a new wave of therapies.
detail: CRISPR (originally a bacterial defense system) lets researchers rewrite the genome with unprecedented accuracy. Casgevy (exa-cel), the first CRISPR-based therapy approved by the FDA in 2023, treats sickle cell disease and beta-thalassemia. You'll hear CRISPR in genetic-disease pipeline discussions, in any biotech IPO involving Editas, Beam, Verve, or Intellia, and in ethical debates about germline editing.

snappy: A specialist company that runs clinical trials for drug companies — handling the operational complexity so the sponsor can focus on the science.
detail: CROs like IQVIA, ICON, and Labcorp design protocols, recruit patients, collect data, and submit to regulators on behalf of pharma sponsors — at scale, they're how trials actually happen. The industry is multi-billion-dollar and highly consolidated. You'll hear CRO in commercial discussions of trial timelines, in M&A activity, and in any drug-development pitch where the sponsor doesn't have in-house clinical operations.

snappy: An immune checkpoint that holds T-cells back from attacking — and the first one ever drugged successfully, kicking off the modern immunotherapy era.
detail: CTLA-4 normally prevents the immune system from attacking the body's own tissues; tumors exploit this brake to evade immune attack. Yervoy (ipilimumab), approved in 2011, was the first checkpoint inhibitor and is now combined with PD-1 drugs in melanoma and other cancers. You'll hear CTLA-4 in any immuno-oncology pipeline discussion, often as the older sibling of PD-1.

snappy: Cell-killing — the formal word for traditional chemotherapy drugs that destroy fast-dividing cells, including cancer (and unfortunately some healthy ones).
detail: Cytotoxic agents include classic chemo drugs like cisplatin, doxorubicin, and paclitaxel — they damage DNA or microtubules to kill dividing cells. The term distinguishes these from cytostatic drugs (which stop growth without killing), targeted therapies, and immunotherapies. You'll hear cytotoxic in trial-design discussions, ADC payload conversations (the "warhead" is usually cytotoxic), and in any conversation contrasting chemo with newer drug classes.

snappy: A clinical trial that lets patients participate from home — using telehealth, wearables, and home-shipped meds — instead of traveling to a research center.
detail: DCTs grew during COVID and stuck around because they cut patient burden, broaden geographic recruiting, and reduce dropouts. Pure-DCT trials are rare; most are hybrid. You'll hear DCT in trial-design discussions, in pitches from companies like Medable and Science 37, and in commercial conversations about including underserved populations.

snappy: A new class of drugs that don't just block a harmful protein — they tag it for the cell's own waste-disposal system to destroy it entirely.
detail: Targeted protein degraders (PROTACs, molecular glues) recruit the cell's ubiquitin system to mark and destroy disease-driving proteins. The class extends drug development to previously "undruggable" proteins. You'll hear degrader at biotech conferences, in oncology and immunology pipelines from Arvinas, C4 Therapeutics, and Kymera, and in any commercial framing of next-generation small molecules.

snappy: A devastating childhood brain tumor that grows in a part of the brainstem too critical for surgery — historically nearly always fatal.
detail: DIPG affects 200-400 children a year in the US, with median survival under a year despite radiation. The location prevents biopsy or resection. Recent trials with ONC201 and CAR-T directed at the H3K27M mutation common in DIPG have shown the first signs of progress in decades. You'll hear DIPG in pediatric oncology fundraising, rare-disease biotech pitches, and patient-advocacy contexts.

snappy: The most common aggressive lymphoma — fast-growing but often curable with combination chemotherapy and antibody drugs.
detail: DLBCL accounts for around 30% of non-Hodgkin lymphomas, traditionally treated with R-CHOP (rituximab plus chemo) with cure rates around 60%. For relapsed/refractory cases, CAR-T therapies (Yescarta, Breyanzi) and ADCs (Polivy) have changed the landscape. You'll hear DLBCL at ASH, in CAR-T pipeline expansions, and in commercial discussions of frontline lymphoma combinations.

snappy: An independent group of experts that reviews trial data while a study is running — and can stop the trial if patients are being harmed or already clearly benefiting.
detail: DMCs (sometimes DSMBs) protect trial participants by watching for safety signals or overwhelming efficacy. Their interim analyses can halt trials early — sometimes for the right reason (drug works, give it to everyone), sometimes the wrong (futility). You'll hear DMC decisions move stocks when they recommend stopping a trial early for benefit, and in ethics-committee reviews of late-stage protocols.

snappy: The body's spell-checker for DNA — when it breaks, mutations pile up uncorrected and cancer risk rises sharply.
detail: MMR deficiency creates microsatellite instability (MSI-H), a state of hypermutation that produces tumors visible to the immune system — which is why MSI-H/dMMR cancers respond unusually well to immunotherapy like Keytruda. Inherited MMR mutations cause Lynch syndrome. You'll hear MMR/dMMR in colorectal and endometrial cancer trial designs and in any precision-immunotherapy pipeline discussion.

snappy: The careful step-by-step approach in early trials of giving small groups of patients increasing doses of a new drug until side effects become unacceptable.
detail: Dose escalation is the heart of Phase 1 trials. Standard designs (3+3, BOIN, mTPI) treat tiny cohorts at each dose level, watching for dose-limiting toxicities, before deciding whether to escalate further. The maximum tolerated dose (MTD) is the headline output, though modern oncology has shifted toward optimal biological dose. You'll hear dose escalation in Phase 1 readouts and at ASCO/AACR poster sessions.

snappy: When one drug changes how another drug works in your body — sometimes making it weaker, sometimes dangerously stronger.
detail: DDIs typically occur through liver enzymes (CYP450) or transporter proteins. Some interactions are mild; others are serious enough to require dose adjustments or to ban combinations entirely. You'll see DDI sections in every drug label, hear them at FDA reviews of polypharmacy populations, and in any commercial discussion of competitor drugs that share metabolic pathways.

snappy: A software-based treatment — usually an app — designed to prevent or manage a medical condition, sometimes prescribed by a doctor like a pill.
detail: Digital therapeutics deliver evidence-based interventions through software, ranging from cognitive behavioral therapy apps for insomnia to attention-training games for ADHD. Some are FDA-cleared and prescription-only — known as "PDTs" (Prescription Digital Therapeutics) — while others are over-the-counter. You'll hear DTx in pharma earnings calls when companies pitch software as the next growth frontier, and at health-tech conferences where pilots are being run with insurers.

snappy: How long a drug keeps working in patients who initially respond to it — a measure of staying power, not just whether tumors shrink at first.
detail: DoR is a key efficacy metric especially in oncology — a drug that produces brief responses isn't as valuable as one delivering durable ones. The metric is reported alongside overall response rate (ORR) in trial readouts. You'll hear DoR in any drug pitch claiming durable benefit, in regulatory discussions of accelerated-approval confirmations, and in commercial differentiation between drugs with similar response rates.

snappy: Abnormally growing cells that aren't cancer yet but are well on their way — a warning sign that gets caught and treated early when possible.
detail: Dysplasia describes disorganized cell growth — graded as low or high — that often precedes invasive cancer. It's commonly found in cervical, esophageal, and colon screening, where treatment of high-grade dysplasia prevents progression to cancer. You'll hear dysplasia in pathology reports, in screening-program discussions, and in any commercial conversation about cancer prevention vs. treatment.

snappy: A cell-surface receptor that, when mutated, drives uncontrolled growth — found in about 15% of lung cancers, plus head-and-neck and colon cancer.
detail: EGFR mutations are the foundation of lung-cancer precision medicine. Drugs like Tagrisso (osimertinib) target EGFR-mutant non-small-cell lung cancer with dramatic response rates; the T790M mutation is a famous resistance pathway that drove second-generation drug development. You'll hear EGFR everywhere in lung-cancer trial readouts, ASCO commercial discussions, and pipeline conversations about overcoming resistance.

snappy: The FDA's European counterpart — the body that approves drugs and biologics for use across the EU.
detail: EMA reviews go through committees (CHMP for human medicines), with national-level reimbursement decisions following separately in each member state. EMA approval often lags FDA by 6-12 months, which matters commercially. You'll hear EMA in any global drug-launch sequencing discussion, at investor calls breaking out US vs. EU revenue projections, and in regulatory affairs strategy.

snappy: Cancer of the lining of the uterus — the most common gynecologic cancer in developed countries, often linked to obesity and hormonal factors.
detail: Endometrial cancer is increasingly classified by molecular subtype (POLE-mutant, MSI-H/dMMR, p53-mutant, no-specific-profile), each with different prognoses and treatment paths. Keytruda is approved for MSI-H endometrial cancer; Lenvima (lenvatinib) plus Keytruda is another important combination. You'll hear it at gynecologic oncology meetings and in immunotherapy expansion narratives.

snappy: What a clinical trial actually measures to decide if a drug works — survival, tumor shrinkage, time to progression, or quality of life.
detail: Endpoints are picked carefully because they determine what the trial proves. Overall survival (OS) is the gold standard but takes years; progression-free survival (PFS) and overall response rate (ORR) are faster surrogates often used for accelerated approval. You'll hear endpoint everywhere in pharma — in trial design, in FDA discussions, and in any earnings call where management defends or explains a topline readout.

snappy: A relatively rare brain or spinal cord tumor that arises from cells lining the fluid-filled cavities of the central nervous system.
detail: Ependymomas are graded I to III by aggressiveness and treated primarily with surgery and radiation. Pediatric posterior-fossa ependymomas are the most studied subset. The disease has limited targeted-therapy options, which makes it an active area of pediatric oncology research. You'll hear ependymoma in pediatric brain-tumor trial readouts and rare-disease drug development contexts.

snappy: Hormone receptors found on most breast cancer cells — when present, they make tumors vulnerable to anti-hormone drugs that block or starve them.
detail: About 70% of breast cancers are ER-positive (and usually PR-positive too), meaning estrogen drives their growth. Treatment includes tamoxifen, aromatase inhibitors, and SERDs like Faslodex (fulvestrant), often combined with CDK4/6 inhibitors in metastatic disease. You'll hear ER/PR at every breast-cancer trial readout and in commercial discussions of the largest commercial segment in oncology.

snappy: Another name for the HER2 gene — and a key driver in about 20% of breast cancers, plus subsets of stomach, lung, and colon cancers.
detail: ERBB2 amplification or overexpression makes tumors vulnerable to HER2-targeted therapies — Herceptin (trastuzumab), Perjeta (pertuzumab), Kadcyla, and Enhertu. Even "HER2-low" tumors are now eligible for some of these drugs. You'll hear ERBB2 in pathology reports and in commercial discussions of the rapidly expanding HER2-targeted franchise.

snappy: Cancer of the esophagus — the tube connecting throat to stomach — with two main types tied to very different risk factors.
detail: Adenocarcinoma is associated with chronic acid reflux and Barrett's esophagus, while squamous cell carcinoma is linked to smoking and alcohol. Treatment is multimodal — surgery, chemoradiation, and increasingly immunotherapy with Keytruda or Opdivo. You'll hear esophageal cancer at GI oncology meetings, in commercial discussions of immunotherapy expansion, and in screening-program advocacy.

snappy: A rare bone and soft-tissue cancer that mostly affects teenagers and young adults — driven by a single, almost-defining gene fusion.
detail: Nearly all Ewing sarcomas carry the EWSR1-FLI1 (or related) gene fusion. Treatment is intense chemotherapy plus surgery and/or radiation, with cure rates around 70% in localized disease. The single fusion makes it a long-running target for novel therapies. You'll hear Ewing in pediatric and adolescent oncology contexts and in rare-disease biotech pipelines.

snappy: Everything in a pill that isn't the actual drug — the binders, fillers, coatings, and preservatives that turn a chemical into something you can swallow.
detail: Excipients give a drug its shape, stability, taste, and release profile. They're regulated alongside the API, since impurities or interactions can affect product safety. You'll hear excipient in formulation discussions, manufacturing-quality reviews, and in any drug-development pitch involving novel delivery (e.g., long-acting injectables, oral solutions for pediatric patients).

snappy: An enzyme that controls which genes get turned on or off — when mutated, it scrambles cellular instructions and helps drive certain blood cancers.
detail: EZH2 mutations are common in follicular lymphoma and DLBCL, leading to inappropriate gene silencing. Tazverik (tazemetostat) is an FDA-approved EZH2 inhibitor for follicular lymphoma and epithelioid sarcoma. You'll hear EZH2 in epigenetic-therapy pipeline discussions and at hematology conferences as part of the broader interest in epigenetic-target oncology.

snappy: An inherited cancer syndrome where people develop hundreds to thousands of polyps in the colon — virtually all of which will turn into cancer if untreated.
detail: FAP is caused by mutations in the APC gene; affected people typically need preventive removal of the colon. The condition affects roughly 1 in 10,000 people. You'll hear FAP in hereditary-cancer genetic counseling discussions, in pediatric/young-adult oncology screening contexts, and as a model condition in colorectal cancer prevention research.

snappy: An FDA designation that speeds up review of drugs targeting serious conditions with unmet needs — letting companies submit pieces of their application as they finish, rather than all at once.
detail: Fast Track allows for rolling reviews and more frequent FDA meetings; it doesn't change the criteria for approval, just the pace. About 30% of new approvals carry Fast Track designation. You'll hear Fast Track in biotech press releases (it's positive news), in regulatory affairs strategy, and on earnings calls when companies discuss expected approval timelines.

snappy: The US agency that decides whether new drugs, vaccines, devices, and food additives are safe and effective enough to be sold in America.
detail: FDA reviews most pharmaceutical applications through CDER (drugs) or CBER (biologics, vaccines, gene therapy), with action goals set by PDUFA. The agency's decisions move billions in market value and define which products doctors can prescribe. You'll hear FDA constantly in pharma — every approval announcement, every Complete Response Letter, every advisory committee meeting is news.

snappy: A family of cell-surface receptors with mutations and gene fusions in several cancers — and the target of a small but growing class of precision drugs.
detail: FGFR2 fusions drive a subset of cholangiocarcinoma; FGFR3 mutations are common in bladder cancer and a rare bone disorder called achondroplasia. Approved FGFR inhibitors include Pemazyre (pemigatinib), Truseltiq (infigratinib), and Balversa (erdafitinib). You'll hear FGFR in rare-cancer trial readouts and in commercial discussions of how mutation-based eligibility creates niche markets.

snappy: The very first time a new drug is given to people — a high-stakes Phase 1 study where everything is unknown until it isn't.
detail: FIH studies typically dose tiny cohorts of healthy volunteers or patients with carefully escalating amounts, watching for safety signals. Famous failures (TGN1412, BIA 10-2474) have made FIH design more cautious. You'll hear FIH in early biotech pipeline discussions, in conference posters reporting first safety data, and in any pitch where a novel mechanism is reaching humans for the first time.

snappy: The treatment doctors try first when a patient is diagnosed — usually the regimen with the best track record for that disease and stage.
detail: If first-line fails, second- and third-line therapies follow. The first-line setting is commercially valuable because it captures the largest patient population; companies fight hard to win first-line indications and resist getting pushed into later lines. You'll hear first-line everywhere in oncology — in trial design, FDA labels, and earnings-call discussions of market positioning.

snappy: A signaling receptor mutated in about 30% of acute myeloid leukemia patients — a marker of more aggressive disease and a target of specific drugs.
detail: FLT3-ITD and FLT3-TKD mutations drive proliferation in AML and historically meant worse prognosis. Targeted FLT3 inhibitors — Rydapt (midostaurin), Xospata (gilteritinib), Vanflyta (quizartinib) — added to standard treatment have improved outcomes meaningfully. You'll hear FLT3 at ASH, in AML trial readouts, and in any commercial discussion of mutation-stratified leukemia therapy.

snappy: The second-most-common non-Hodgkin lymphoma — slow-growing, generally treatable but rarely curable, with patients living many years through repeated treatments.
detail: Follicular lymphoma usually carries the BCL2 translocation that prevents cancer cells from dying. Treatment options include rituximab plus chemo, EZH2 inhibitor Tazverik (tazemetostat), CAR-T (Yescarta), and bispecifics like Lunsumio (mosunetuzumab). You'll hear follicular lymphoma at ASH and in any pipeline pitch around B-cell malignancies.

snappy: The list of drugs your insurance will pay for — and the rules that decide what it costs you when you fill a prescription.
detail: Insurers and pharmacy benefit managers (PBMs) build formularies to control costs, negotiating rebates with drugmakers in exchange for preferred placement. A drug's tier on the formulary determines patient copays, prior-authorization requirements, and ultimately how easily it gets prescribed. Pharma launch teams obsess over formulary access — at JPMorgan and on earnings calls you'll hear it called "access" or "reimbursement," and it's often the difference between a drug's commercial success and failure.

snappy: The specific recipe and physical form of a drug product — pill, injection, patch, inhaler — and the choices that affect how (and whether) it works.
detail: Formulation determines bioavailability, dosing schedule, patient adherence, and shelf life. Reformulating a drug (e.g., from IV to subcutaneous, or from twice-daily to once-daily) can be a major commercial play and patent-extension strategy. You'll hear formulation in late-stage drug development, lifecycle-management discussions, and in any pitch involving long-acting injectables or improved patient experience.

snappy: A hybrid gene formed when two normally separate genes accidentally join together — often producing an abnormal protein that drives cancer.
detail: Fusion genes — BCR-ABL in CML, EML4-ALK in lung cancer, EWSR1-FLI1 in Ewing sarcoma, NTRK fusions across many tumors — are powerful drug targets because they're cancer-specific. Targeted therapies like Gleevec, Xalkori, and Vitrakvi (larotrectinib) all hit fusion proteins. You'll hear fusion gene in precision-oncology trial readouts and in commercial pitches around tumor-agnostic biomarker-driven approvals.

snappy: A rare and usually late-diagnosed cancer of the small organ that stores bile — most often discovered incidentally during gallstone surgery.
detail: Gallbladder cancer is uncommon in the US but more frequent in parts of South America and South Asia. HER2 amplification occurs in a subset, opening targeted therapy options like Herceptin and ADCs. You'll hear gallbladder cancer in biliary-tract oncology contexts and in expanding HER2-tumor-agnostic discussions.

snappy: Stomach cancer — a major killer worldwide, especially in East Asia, with subtypes that respond differently to modern targeted and immune drugs.
detail: Gastric cancer is classified into intestinal and diffuse (signet ring) types, with HER2-positive, MSI-H, and EBV-positive subsets carrying targeted-therapy implications. Keytruda, Opdivo, Herceptin, and Enhertu all have approved roles. You'll hear gastric cancer at GI oncology meetings, in immuno-oncology global expansion stories, and in HER2 lifecycle discussions.

snappy: International quality standards that govern how clinical trials must be designed, conducted, and reported — to protect patients and produce trustworthy data.
detail: GCP guidelines (codified by ICH) cover everything from informed consent to data integrity to investigator qualifications. FDA inspectors audit trial sites for GCP compliance; violations can invalidate data and tank approvals. You'll hear GCP in any clinical operations role, in regulatory inspections, and in CRO selection discussions.

snappy: Treatments that work by adding, replacing, or correcting genes inside cells — aiming to fix the root cause of a genetic disease rather than manage its symptoms.
detail: Approved gene therapies include Luxturna (RPE65 mutations causing blindness), Zolgensma (SMA), Hemgenix (hemophilia B), and the CRISPR-based Casgevy (sickle cell). One-time price tags often exceed $2 million, raising payer-access questions. You'll hear gene therapy in rare-disease biotech pipelines, at investor calls about durable-response economics, and in payer-access debates.

snappy: A copy of a brand-name pill containing the same active ingredient — sold at much lower prices once the original patent expires.
detail: Generic drugs are approved through abbreviated NDAs (ANDAs) by demonstrating bioequivalence to the reference product. They make up about 90% of US prescriptions but only around 20% of drug spending. Generic competition can collapse a brand's revenue overnight. You'll hear generics in patent-cliff discussions, payer formulary contracts, and antitrust debates.

snappy: Cancer that arises from reproductive cells — most often in the testicles or ovaries, but occasionally elsewhere if those cells migrated during fetal development.
detail: Germ cell tumors are notable as the cancer most likely to be cured even at advanced stages, especially testicular cancer cured with cisplatin-based chemotherapy. Subtypes include seminomas/dysgerminomas and non-seminomatous tumors. You'll hear germ cell tumor in testicular and ovarian cancer contexts and in pediatric/young-adult oncology research.

snappy: A rare sarcoma of the digestive tract — and one of oncology's biggest precision-medicine wins after Gleevec turned a deadly cancer into a manageable disease.
detail: Most GISTs carry KIT or PDGFRA mutations, making them dramatically responsive to imatinib (Gleevec) and successor drugs Sutent (sunitinib), Stivarga (regorafenib), and Qinlock (ripretinib). The cancer is the textbook proof that targeted therapy works in solid tumors. You'll hear GIST in sarcoma research and in any commercial discussion of KIT-driven oncology.

snappy: The most aggressive brain cancer — fast-growing, deeply infiltrating, and despite intensive treatment, currently incurable in nearly all cases.
detail: GBM has median survival of about 15 months despite surgery, radiation, and temozolomide chemotherapy. Tumor Treating Fields (Optune) is one of the few additions in decades. The disease is a perennial focus of biotech-and-academic effort, but most trials have failed. You'll hear GBM in neuro-oncology research, in patient-advocacy fundraising, and in commercial pitches highlighting an underserved indication.

snappy: The umbrella name for brain tumors arising from glial cells — the supporting cells of the nervous system — graded by aggressiveness from 1 to 4.
detail: Gliomas include astrocytomas, oligodendrogliomas, and ependymomas, each with distinct genetics. The 2021 WHO classification reorganized them around mutations like IDH and 1p/19q codeletion rather than just appearance. Targeted therapies like Voranigo (vorasidenib) for IDH-mutant glioma are reshaping treatment. You'll hear glioma in neuro-oncology discussions and in the steady flow of brain-cancer biotech pitches.

snappy: A drug that mimics a natural gut hormone to lower blood sugar and curb appetite — the engine behind the Ozempic and Wegovy phenomenon.
detail: GLP-1 receptor agonists — Ozempic (semaglutide), Mounjaro (tirzepatide, dual GLP-1/GIP), Trulicity (dulaglutide), Wegovy — were originally approved for type 2 diabetes, then revolutionized obesity treatment with weight loss of 15-20%. New trials show benefit in heart failure, kidney disease, and possibly Alzheimer's. You'll hear GLP-1 dominate every Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly earnings call, and at JPMorgan it's the single most-discussed therapeutic class — supply, oral formulations, and label expansions are the running storylines.

snappy: The quality standards drug manufacturers must follow to make sure every batch is safe, pure, and consistent — enforced by FDA inspections.
detail: GMP covers facility design, equipment, processes, documentation, and personnel training. Failing a GMP inspection can result in Warning Letters, import bans, or recalls. You'll hear GMP in supply-chain discussions, manufacturing-deviation reviews, and at the FDA's annual list of Form 483 observations and Warning Letters.

snappy: A pathologist's measure of how abnormal cancer cells look under a microscope — higher grades mean more aggressive disease.
detail: Grade is distinct from stage (which describes how far the cancer has spread). Most cancers use a 1-3 or 1-4 grade scale; high-grade tumors generally grow faster and require more intensive treatment. You'll see grade in pathology reports, oncology trial enrollment criteria, and in any treatment-decision discussion at tumor boards.

snappy: The gap between a drug's list price and what the manufacturer actually keeps after rebates, discounts, fees, and chargebacks — often half of revenue or more.
detail: Gross-to-net adjustments are the deductions pharma takes off list price before booking real revenue: PBM rebates, 340B discounts, Medicaid mandatory rebates, distributor fees, and patient assistance. For drugs like Humira (adalimumab) or Eliquis (apixaban) the GTN bubble can exceed 50%. You'll hear analysts grill CFOs on "net pricing" at every earnings call, and it's the single biggest line item between a drug's headline price and what reaches the bottom line.

snappy: How long it takes for half of a drug to be eliminated from your body — a critical number that determines how often you need to take it.
detail: Drugs with short half-lives (a few hours) need frequent dosing; drugs with long half-lives (days or weeks) can be dosed once a week, monthly, or even less often. Half-life shapes patient adherence and competitive positioning. You'll hear half-life in formulation discussions, in long-acting injectable pitches, and in any commercial framing of dosing convenience.

snappy: A statistical number comparing the risk of an event happening between a treatment group and a control group — under 1.0 means the treatment helps.
detail: An HR of 0.7 means a 30% reduction in the rate of the event (e.g., death or progression). Hazard ratios are the standard way oncology trials report time-to-event endpoints; they're paired with confidence intervals and p-values. You'll see HR in every trial readout, on Kaplan-Meier curves, and in any FDA review summary.

snappy: The most common type of liver cancer — strongly tied to chronic hepatitis B/C infection or cirrhosis from alcohol or fatty-liver disease.
detail: HCC treatment depends on stage and liver function. Early-stage disease is treated with surgery, ablation, or transplant; advanced disease relies on combinations like Tecentriq (atezolizumab) plus Avastin, or Imjudo (tremelimumab) plus Imfinzi. You'll hear HCC in liver-cancer trial readouts and in commercial discussions of immunotherapy expansion in solid tumors.

snappy: Cancers of the mouth, throat, larynx, and related regions — historically tied to smoking and alcohol, increasingly driven by HPV.
detail: HPV-positive head and neck cancers respond better to treatment than HPV-negative ones, prompting de-escalation trials. Treatment combines surgery, radiation, chemo, and immunotherapy with Keytruda or Opdivo. You'll hear HNSCC at oncology meetings, in HPV-vaccination policy debates, and in commercial discussions of immunotherapy expansion.

snappy: A condition where the heart can't pump enough blood to meet the body's needs — not the same as a heart attack, and increasingly manageable as a chronic disease.
detail: Heart failure is split into reduced (HFrEF) and preserved (HFpEF) ejection fraction — for years only HFrEF had effective drugs, but SGLT2 inhibitors like Jardiance (empagliflozin) changed that. The four-pillar regimen (ARNI, beta blocker, MRA, SGLT2) is now guideline-standard. You'll hear heart failure framed on Boehringer, AstraZeneca, and Novartis earnings calls as a multi-billion-dollar growth story driven by HFpEF expansion, and at AHA and ESC when trial readouts redefine standard of care.

snappy: The umbrella term for cancers of the blood, bone marrow, and lymphatic system — leukemias, lymphomas, and myelomas.
detail: Hematologic malignancies behave very differently from solid tumors and have driven much of the past decade's innovation: BTK inhibitors, BCL-2 inhibitors, CAR-T, bispecifics. ASH (December) is the field's defining conference. You'll hear hematologic malignancy in any trial-design discussion that distinguishes from solid tumors, and in commercial pipeline framing.

snappy: The discipline that translates a drug's clinical benefit into dollars and quality-of-life numbers — building the case payers actually want to see before they'll cover it.
detail: HEOR teams produce the evidence pharma uses in formulary submissions, ICER reviews, and payer negotiations: cost-effectiveness models, budget-impact analyses, and real-world outcomes data. Quality-adjusted life years (QALYs) are a common currency, especially in markets like the UK's NICE. You'll hear HEOR cited at every launch readiness review and ISPOR conference, and in commercial discussions of how to position a drug against existing treatments on value, not just clinical results.

snappy: A protein on the surface of cells that tells them to grow — when overexpressed in breast or stomach cancer, it makes the disease aggressive but also targetable.
detail: HER2 amplification occurs in about 20% of breast cancers and around 15% of gastric cancers. Herceptin (trastuzumab) was the first targeted therapy here, followed by Perjeta, Kadcyla, and the ADC Enhertu (trastuzumab deruxtecan), which has expanded into HER2-low tumors. You'll hear HER2 at every breast-cancer trial readout and in any commercial discussion of the HER2 franchise.

snappy: Inherited gene mutations that pass cancer risk down through families — the reason some people develop multiple cancers, or get them young.
detail: Common hereditary syndromes include Lynch syndrome (colon, endometrial), BRCA1/BRCA2 (breast, ovarian, prostate, pancreatic), Li-Fraumeni (TP53, many cancers), and FAP (colon polyposis). Genetic testing influences screening intensity and treatment selection — including PARP-inhibitor eligibility. You'll hear hereditary cancer syndromes in genetic counseling and in any precision-oncology pipeline discussion.

snappy: A cancer of B-cells with a distinct microscope appearance — historically among the most curable cancers, even at advanced stages.
detail: Hodgkin lymphoma is defined by Reed-Sternberg cells. Treatment with combination chemo (ABVD, BV-AVD) and the ADC Adcetris (brentuximab vedotin), often with checkpoint inhibitors for relapsed disease, achieves cure rates above 80%. You'll hear Hodgkin in lymphoma research, in long-term-survivorship discussions about late effects, and as a benchmark cancer for combination-therapy strategies.

snappy: Proteins on certain cancer cells that bind to hormones (mostly estrogen or progesterone) — when present, they make the tumor sensitive to anti-hormone treatments.
detail: In breast cancer, hormone-receptor status (ER+, PR+, or both) defines a major subtype that's treated with tamoxifen, aromatase inhibitors, or SERDs alongside CDK4/6 inhibitors. Prostate cancer is similarly hormone-driven, treated with androgen-deprivation therapy. You'll hear HR/HR+ at every breast-cancer discussion and in any commercial discussion of endocrine-therapy lifecycle management.

snappy: A common virus that causes cervical cancer and a growing share of head-and-neck cancers — and is largely preventable with vaccination.
detail: HPV is responsible for nearly all cervical cancers and a rising fraction of oropharyngeal cancers. The Gardasil 9 vaccine prevents the highest-risk strains and is recommended for adolescents. The result has been a measurable drop in cervical cancer rates wherever vaccination is widespread. You'll hear HPV in oncology prevention discussions and in vaccination-policy debates.

snappy: A defect in the cell's ability to fix a specific kind of DNA damage — and a marker that predicts which tumors will respond well to PARP inhibitors.
detail: HRD is most often caused by BRCA mutations but extends to other DNA-repair genes (ATM, PALB2, RAD51). HRD-positive ovarian cancers and a subset of breast and prostate cancers respond particularly well to Lynparza (olaparib), Zejula (niraparib), and similar drugs. You'll hear HRD in precision-oncology trial readouts and in commercial discussions of biomarker-driven drug selection.

snappy: Chronically high blood pressure — the silent driver of heart attacks, strokes, and kidney failure that affects nearly half of US adults.
detail: Hypertension is treated with a stepwise stack: ACE inhibitors or ARBs first, often layered with diuretics, calcium-channel blockers, and beta blockers. Most drugs are generic and cheap, making it a low-margin commercial space. You'll hear hypertension less on earnings calls than in policy commentary — uncontrolled blood pressure is the biggest preventable driver of US cardiovascular disease, and population-health programs at insurers and integrated systems live or die on their hypertension numbers.

snappy: Low oxygen levels — a condition tumors create as they outgrow their blood supply, paradoxically making them harder to kill with radiation and chemo.
detail: Hypoxic tumor regions resist treatment because radiation needs oxygen to damage DNA effectively, and many drugs reach hypoxic areas poorly. Tumor hypoxia also drives angiogenesis and metastasis through HIF-1α signaling. You'll hear hypoxia in radiation-oncology discussions, in advanced-tumor biology research, and in any drug-development pitch around hypoxia-activated prodrugs.

snappy: Genes encoding metabolic enzymes — when mutated, they produce a cancer-causing molecule that scrambles cell development. Found in some leukemias, brain tumors, and bile-duct cancers.
detail: IDH-mutant cancers produce 2-hydroxyglutarate, an "oncometabolite" that disrupts normal gene regulation. Tibsovo (ivosidenib) and Idhifa (enasidenib) target IDH1 and IDH2 mutations in AML; Voranigo (vorasidenib) was approved for IDH-mutant glioma in 2024. You'll hear IDH at hematology and neuro-oncology meetings, and in any precision-oncology pipeline discussion.

snappy: The first true targeted cancer therapy — approved in 2001 for CML, and still the textbook proof that designing drugs against specific mutations works.
detail: Imatinib blocks BCR-ABL in CML, KIT in GIST, and PDGFRA in some sarcomas. It transformed CML from a fatal disease into a manageable chronic condition with a daily pill. The drug's commercial success and cultural impact made precision oncology mainstream. You'll hear Gleevec referenced as the historical benchmark in any targeted-therapy pitch.

snappy: The biological 'off-switches' that keep your immune system from attacking your own body — and that tumors hijack to hide from immune attack.
detail: Immune checkpoints — PD-1, PD-L1, CTLA-4, LAG-3, TIGIT, TIM-3 — are now major drug targets. Releasing the brakes with checkpoint inhibitors lets T-cells recognize and kill tumors. You'll hear immune checkpoint at every immuno-oncology discussion, at every major oncology conference, and in commercial competition between companies racing for next-generation targets.

snappy: Treatments that harness the immune system to fight cancer — by removing brakes, training T-cells, or engineering immune cells from scratch.
detail: Immunotherapy includes checkpoint inhibitors, CAR-T, bispecific antibodies, cancer vaccines, cytokines, and adoptive cell therapies. It has transformed melanoma, lung cancer, and several blood cancers. The category is now the dominant theme in oncology drug development. You'll hear immunotherapy across all of oncology, especially around combination strategies trying to expand who responds.

snappy: The application a company files with the FDA to start testing a new drug in humans — the regulatory gate that opens Phase 1 trials.
detail: An IND submission includes preclinical data, manufacturing details (CMC), and the clinical-trial plan. The FDA has 30 days to object; if they don't, the trial proceeds. "Filing an IND" is a meaningful milestone that biotechs announce. You'll hear IND in early-stage pipeline discussions and at investor briefings on near-term clinical-development milestones.
