snappy: The specific disease or condition a drug is approved to treat — listed on the FDA-approved label.
detail: A drug's indication defines its commercial market and what doctors can prescribe legally on-label. Companies pursue "label expansion" by running new trials to add indications, often in adjacent cancer types or earlier lines of therapy. You'll hear indication in every commercial discussion, regulatory update, and in trial-design conversations about which patient populations to enroll.

snappy: An umbrella term for chronic conditions — Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis — where the immune system attacks the digestive tract, causing pain, bleeding, and severe diarrhea.
detail: IBD treatment ranges from steroids and 5-ASAs to biologics like Humira (adalimumab), Stelara (ustekinumab), and the IL-23 class led by Skyrizi (risankizumab) and Tremfya (guselkumab). Patients often cycle through multiple biologics as drugs lose effect over years. You'll hear IBD on AbbVie, J&J, and Takeda earnings calls as a multi-billion-dollar autoimmune market, and at Digestive Disease Week (DDW) when head-to-head trial readouts shake up prescribing patterns.

snappy: The legal and ethical promise that patients in a clinical trial understand the risks, benefits, and alternatives — and agree freely, in writing, to participate.
detail: Informed consent is the cornerstone of human-subjects research, codified after the abuses of Tuskegee and Nuremberg. Patients receive a document — sometimes 30+ pages — covering everything from study procedures to potential side effects, and can withdraw at any time. You'll see informed consent surface in regulatory inspections, trial-misconduct lawsuits, and any commercial discussion of patient recruitment, since dense or rushed consent processes are a leading cause of FDA warning letters.

snappy: A drug that blocks a protein from doing its job — most cancer drugs are inhibitors of one enzyme or receptor or another.
detail: Inhibitors come in many flavors: small-molecule kinase inhibitors (Gleevec, Tagrisso), antibody-based receptor blockers (Herceptin), enzyme inhibitors (PARP inhibitors). The common thread is shutting down a specific protein the cancer depends on. You'll hear "X inhibitor" used to label drug classes across every pharma pipeline and trial readout.

snappy: The hormone your pancreas makes to move sugar out of your blood and into your cells — and the lifesaving drug people with diabetes use when their bodies can't.
detail: Insulin has been pharma since Banting's 1921 discovery, with modern forms ranging from short-acting Humalog (lispro) to ultra-long Tresiba (degludec). Pricing became a political flashpoint in the 2010s after list prices tripled, and the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act capped Medicare insulin copays at $35. You'll hear insulin in every diabetes earnings call, in coverage debates, and at hearings on PBM and manufacturer pricing — it's the textbook example of US drug-pricing controversy.

snappy: A trial-analysis rule that counts every patient in the group they were originally assigned to, even if they dropped out or switched treatments midway.
detail: ITT analysis preserves randomization — the foundation of clinical-trial credibility — by refusing to cherry-pick the patients who actually completed treatment. The opposite, "per-protocol," can inflate apparent benefit by excluding non-compliers. Regulators almost always demand ITT as the primary analysis for confirmatory trials. You'll hear it on trial readouts at ASCO and in earnings calls when companies parse why a per-protocol number looked stronger than the headline ITT result.

snappy: Injected directly into a tumor — a delivery approach that concentrates drug where it's needed and minimizes whole-body side effects.
detail: Intratumoral delivery is used for some immunotherapies (oncolytic viruses like Imlygic), STING agonists, and TLR agonists, where the goal is local immune activation that then triggers a body-wide response. The approach has obvious limits — accessibility of the tumor — but is a major commercial bet for several biotechs. You'll hear intratumoral in immuno-oncology pipelines and at AACR/SITC.

snappy: The most common type of breast cancer — about 80% of cases — arising from cells in the milk ducts and breaking through into surrounding tissue.
detail: IDC is what most people mean by 'breast cancer.' It's classified by hormone-receptor status, HER2 status, and grade, with treatment determined by those features. Invasive lobular carcinoma is the second most common type. You'll hear IDC in pathology reports and breast-cancer trial enrollment criteria; commercial breast-cancer franchise discussions are essentially about IDC subtypes.

snappy: Drug delivery directly into a vein — bypassing the digestive system entirely and giving you 100% bioavailability immediately.
detail: IV delivery is essential for biologics like antibodies (which would be destroyed in the gut), for emergency settings, and for chemotherapy where precise dosing matters. The cost is patient burden — clinic visits and infusion time — which is why companies push for subcutaneous or oral reformulations as products mature. You'll hear IV vs. SC compared in every biologic-lifecycle discussion.

snappy: A class of drugs that block JAK enzymes — quieting overactive immune signaling in conditions like rheumatoid arthritis and certain blood disorders.
detail: JAK inhibitors include Xeljanz (tofacitinib), Olumiant (baricitinib), Rinvoq (upadacitinib), and Jakafi (ruxolitinib). They're effective in RA, psoriatic arthritis, ulcerative colitis, and myelofibrosis, but carry safety warnings around cardiovascular events and infections. You'll hear JAK inhibitors in immunology pipeline discussions and in any commercial framing of oral biologic alternatives.

snappy: A signaling pathway that runs from cell-surface receptors straight into the nucleus — central to immune responses, blood-cell production, and many cancers.
detail: JAK-STAT signaling translates external signals (cytokines, growth factors) into changes in gene expression. Disruption of the pathway underlies several diseases; modulation by JAK inhibitors is the basis for myelofibrosis treatment and several immunology drugs. You'll hear JAK-STAT in immunology and hematology research, and in any pipeline discussion of cytokine-targeted therapy.

snappy: A specific gene mutation that causes the bone marrow to make too many red blood cells — the defining genetic change in polycythemia vera.
detail: JAK2 V617F is found in nearly all polycythemia vera cases and most cases of essential thrombocythemia and primary myelofibrosis. The mutation is what makes Jakafi (ruxolitinib) effective in these myeloproliferative neoplasms. You'll hear JAK2 V617F in hematology contexts, MPN pipeline discussions, and in any commercial conversation about JAK-targeted therapy.

snappy: A rare and aggressive childhood blood cancer driven by mutations in the RAS signaling pathway — and one of the few pediatric cancers with very limited treatment options.
detail: JMML usually develops before age 4, often associated with NF1, Noonan syndrome, or PTPN11 mutations. Bone-marrow transplant remains the only proven curative therapy, with relapse common. The rarity and aggressiveness make it a focus of pediatric rare-cancer drug development. You'll hear JMML in pediatric oncology research and rare-disease biotech pitches.

snappy: A clinical trial run at multiple sites simultaneously — usually because no single site can recruit enough patients quickly enough.
detail: Joint or multi-center trials are the norm for Phase 2 and Phase 3 studies, especially in rare diseases or specific cancers where any one center sees too few patients. They require coordinated protocols, central data management, and harmonized investigator training. You'll hear joint trial in operational discussions, in CRO contracts, and in any global registration program design.

snappy: A statistical method that draws a step-down curve showing the probability of patients surviving (or staying in remission) over time — the iconic graph in every oncology paper.
detail: Kaplan-Meier curves visualize time-to-event data, comparing treatment arms across months and years. The shape of the curves — and the gap between them — communicates trial results at a glance. You'll see Kaplan-Meier curves on every oncology trial readout slide, in every FDA review document, and in any earnings-call walkthrough of trial results.

snappy: A photo of all of someone's chromosomes laid out in order — a basic test that can spot major chromosomal changes driving disease.
detail: Karyotyping is a foundational technique for identifying chromosomal translocations (like the Philadelphia chromosome of CML), deletions, or extra copies. Modern molecular methods (FISH, NGS) have largely replaced it for fine-grained analysis, but karyotype remains useful for big-picture chromosomal changes. You'll hear karyotype in pediatric and hematologic disease workups.

snappy: A protein found inside dividing cells — pathologists measure how many tumor cells have it to estimate how fast a cancer is growing.
detail: Ki-67 staining gives a percentage; higher percentages mean more aggressive disease. The marker is widely used in breast cancer, lymphoma, and neuroendocrine tumors to guide treatment decisions and prognosis. You'll see Ki-67 in pathology reports, in trial enrollment criteria, and in any commercial discussion of patient stratification.

snappy: Renal cell carcinoma — the most common kidney cancer in adults, often discovered incidentally on imaging and increasingly treatable with immunotherapy combos.
detail: Clear cell RCC, the most common subtype, often carries VHL mutations driving the HIF pathway. Treatment combines anti-VEGF tyrosine kinase inhibitors (Inlyta, Lenvima, Cabometyx) with checkpoint inhibitors. You'll hear RCC at GU oncology meetings (ASCO GU), in immunotherapy combination pipelines, and in commercial discussions of the IO+TKI franchise.

snappy: An enzyme that adds phosphate groups to other proteins — turning them on or off — and a workhorse target of cancer drug development.
detail: Kinases regulate cell growth, division, and signaling; mutated kinases drive many cancers. Kinase inhibitors are one of the largest classes of cancer drugs — Gleevec, Tagrisso, Imbruvica, Verzenio, Tafinlar are all kinase inhibitors. You'll hear kinase in any precision-oncology pipeline discussion and at every major oncology conference.

snappy: A cell-surface receptor that, when mutated, drives gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST) and a handful of other cancers — and the target of imatinib's second great success story.
detail: KIT mutations are the defining feature of most GISTs and a subset of mast-cell diseases and AML. Gleevec was originally developed for CML but turned out to also dramatically treat KIT-driven GIST. Newer KIT inhibitors (Sutent, Stivarga, Qinlock, Ayvakit) handle resistance and additional KIT subtypes. You'll hear KIT in GIST contexts and rare-disease oncology pipelines.

snappy: The most commonly mutated cancer gene of all — and historically the most stubbornly undruggable, until KRAS G12C inhibitors finally cracked it in 2021.
detail: KRAS mutations occur in roughly 30% of all cancers, especially pancreatic (>90%), colorectal, and lung. After decades of failure, Lumakras (sotorasib) and Krazati (adagrasib) became the first FDA-approved drugs targeting KRAS G12C, opening the door to additional KRAS variants. You'll hear KRAS at every oncology conference and in commercial discussions of one of pharma's biggest emerging markets.

snappy: The official document that tells doctors how to prescribe a drug — covering its approved indications, dosing, warnings, and known side effects.
detail: The drug label (officially "prescribing information") is what FDA approves alongside the drug. Every word is negotiated. "Label expansion" — adding indications via supplemental approvals — is a major commercial play. "Off-label" prescribing is legal for doctors but can't be promoted by manufacturers. You'll hear label in every commercial strategy discussion and at every regulatory affairs meeting.

snappy: Cancer of the blood-forming cells in bone marrow — divided into acute or chronic, and lymphoid or myeloid, with very different behaviors and treatments.
detail: The four main leukemias — AML, ALL, CML, CLL — each have distinct genetics, treatment paths, and patient populations. ASH (December) is the central conference. The category has been at the leading edge of treatment innovation, from Gleevec to CAR-T to BCL-2 and BTK inhibitors. You'll hear leukemia constantly in oncology, often as the testing ground for the next mechanism.

snappy: A rare inherited condition where people are born with a broken TP53 gene — leading to extraordinarily high lifetime risk of multiple cancers.
detail: Li-Fraumeni carriers face a near-certain lifetime risk of cancer, often diagnosed young, with sarcomas, breast cancer, brain tumors, and adrenocortical carcinoma being characteristic. Intensive screening (Toronto protocol) has improved outcomes. You'll hear Li-Fraumeni in genetic-counseling discussions and in any rare-cancer or pediatric oncology context where TP53 status matters.

snappy: A molecule that binds to a receptor — like a key fitting a lock — and triggers (or blocks) a biological response.
detail: Ligands include hormones, growth factors, and neurotransmitters in nature, and many drugs in medicine. Pharma drug development largely revolves around designing better artificial ligands for therapeutic receptors. You'll hear ligand in basic pharmacology, in receptor-targeted drug development, and in any pitch involving allosteric vs. orthosteric drug design.

snappy: A blood test that detects fragments of tumor DNA — letting doctors track cancer or spot mutations without an invasive tissue biopsy.
detail: Liquid biopsies look for circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) shed by cancer cells. They're used to identify targetable mutations, monitor treatment response, and detect minimal residual disease (MRD) after treatment. Companies like Guardant Health, Natera, and Foundation Medicine have built large businesses here. You'll hear liquid biopsy in oncology trial monitoring and in commercial discussions of MRD-guided treatment decisions.

snappy: A larger initial dose given to quickly bring drug levels in the body up to where they need to be — followed by smaller maintenance doses.
detail: Loading doses are common in antibiotics, anticoagulants, and biologics with long half-lives, where waiting for steady-state through normal dosing would take days or weeks. The strategy gets the patient to therapeutic levels fast. You'll hear loading dose in clinical pharmacology and in dosing-schedule discussions where rapid onset matters.

snappy: The most common type of lung cancer — and the one most likely to carry targetable genetic mutations like EGFR, ALK, ROS1, and KRAS.
detail: Lung adenocarcinoma accounts for roughly 40% of lung cancers and has driven much of precision oncology. Standard-of-care now includes molecular testing for EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, MET, RET, NTRK, and KRAS G12C — each with approved targeted drugs. You'll hear lung adenocarcinoma at every oncology conference and in any commercial discussion of NSCLC franchise expansion.

snappy: Small bean-shaped immune outposts scattered throughout the body — and often the first place cancer spreads when it leaves the original tumor site.
detail: Lymph nodes filter foreign material and are where immune cells coordinate responses. In cancer, lymph node involvement (signaled by sentinel-node biopsy) is a key staging factor that determines treatment intensity. You'll hear lymph node in every cancer-staging discussion, in surgical oncology, and in early-stage trial design where nodal status defines the population.

snappy: Cancer of the lymphatic system — the tissues and vessels that move immune cells around the body. Includes Hodgkin and the many non-Hodgkin lymphomas.
detail: Lymphomas range from indolent (follicular, MALT) to aggressive (DLBCL, mantle cell, Burkitt). The category has been the proving ground for many recent breakthroughs: rituximab, BTK inhibitors, BCL-2 inhibitors, CAR-T, bispecifics. You'll hear lymphoma at every ASH and at any pharma pipeline discussion of B-cell-targeted therapy.

snappy: An inherited cancer syndrome caused by broken DNA-mismatch-repair genes — sharply raising lifetime risk of colorectal, endometrial, and several other cancers.
detail: Lynch syndrome (formerly hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer) affects roughly 1 in 300 people. Carriers benefit from enhanced screening colonoscopies and may consider risk-reducing surgery. Lynch tumors are MSI-high, making them strongly responsive to checkpoint inhibitors. You'll hear Lynch in genetic-counseling discussions, in colorectal-cancer screening protocols, and in immunotherapy biomarker conversations.

snappy: Freeze-drying — a manufacturing process that removes water from a drug while preserving its structure, producing a stable powder for shipping and storage.
detail: Lyophilization extends the shelf life of biologics, vaccines, and unstable small-molecules that would otherwise degrade. The drug is reconstituted with sterile water before use. You'll hear lyophilization in manufacturing-process discussions, especially around biologics and emergency-stockpile drugs that need long-term storage stability.

snappy: A lab-made antibody designed to lock onto one specific target — and the basis for many of pharma's biggest blockbusters, all ending in '-mab.'
detail: Monoclonal antibodies bind specific proteins, marking them for immune destruction or blocking their function. Examples include Humira (adalimumab), Keytruda (pembrolizumab), Herceptin (trastuzumab), and dozens more. The naming convention (-mab) tells you the modality at a glance. You'll hear mAb in nearly every biologics discussion, at every immunology and oncology conference.

snappy: A lighter-touch treatment given after primary therapy works — to keep cancer in check and delay it coming back.
detail: Maintenance is common in lymphoma (rituximab, lenalidomide), ovarian cancer (PARP inhibitors), and lung cancer (targeted therapy continuation). The strategy aims for prolonged disease control with manageable side effects. You'll hear maintenance therapy in trial-design discussions of progression-free survival and in commercial framing of long-duration treatment.

snappy: Persistent low mood, loss of interest, and physical symptoms like sleep and appetite changes — diagnosed when they last at least two weeks and disrupt daily life.
detail: MDD affects roughly 8% of US adults in any given year and is the leading cause of disability worldwide. First-line treatment is usually SSRIs or SNRIs paired with talk therapy, but newer options include Spravato (esketamine) for treatment-resistant cases and the rapid-acting Auvelity (dextromethorphan-bupropion). You'll hear MDD framed at JPMorgan as one of the largest underserved markets in pharma, and at investor days when companies pitch novel mechanisms beyond the serotonin pathway.

snappy: An aggressive, hard-to-cure non-Hodgkin lymphoma defined by a specific gene translocation that switches on a key cell-cycle regulator.
detail: MCL accounts for ~5% of NHL cases, characterized by t(11;14) leading to cyclin D1 overexpression. Treatment includes intensive chemoimmunotherapy, BTK inhibitors (Imbruvica, Calquence, Brukinsa), and CAR-T (Tecartus). The disease is a frequent subject of next-generation B-cell-malignancy trials. You'll hear MCL at ASH and in BTK-inhibitor lifecycle discussions.

snappy: A protein that normally keeps the tumor-suppressor p53 in check — when MDM2 itself becomes overactive in cancer, it shuts down a critical brake on tumor growth.
detail: MDM2 amplification is common in well-differentiated and dedifferentiated liposarcomas; MDM2 inhibitors aim to release p53 and trigger cancer cell death. Several MDM2 inhibitors are in clinical development. You'll hear MDM2 in sarcoma research and in any pipeline discussion of p53-pathway-restoration therapies.

snappy: A pre-leukemic bone marrow disorder where blood cells don't form properly — often progresses to acute leukemia, especially in older adults.
detail: MDS is treated based on risk score: lower-risk with growth factors or lenalidomide, higher-risk with hypomethylating agents like Vidaza (azacitidine) or Dacogen (decitabine), often progressing to bone-marrow transplant. The IDH and TP53 mutation subsets have led to targeted approvals. You'll hear MDS at ASH, in MDS/AML transition discussions, and in pipeline conversations about hematology pre-leukemic disease.

snappy: How a drug actually works at the molecular level — the specific protein it targets and the biological cascade it sets off.
detail: MOA defines drug class, predicts side effects, and shapes combination strategies. "Novel MOA" is a common biotech selling point in pipeline discussions, as it implies less competitive crowding and potential efficacy in patients who failed existing classes. You'll hear MOA in every pharmacology lecture, every drug-development pitch, and every commercial competitive-intelligence discussion.

snappy: The federal program that pays for prescription drugs for seniors on Medicare — funded jointly by patient premiums, government dollars, and pharma rebates.
detail: Launched in 2006, Part D covers outpatient prescriptions through private plans contracted by CMS — plans negotiate with PBMs, set formularies, and structure tiers. The 2022 Inflation Reduction Act gave Medicare new powers to negotiate prices on top-spend drugs starting 2026, a seismic shift pharma has been fighting in court. You'll hear Part D in every CFO commentary on pricing risk, and it dominates the policy track at JPMorgan.

snappy: The most common malignant brain tumor in children — usually arising from the cerebellum and increasingly classified by molecular subtype rather than appearance.
detail: Medulloblastoma is now divided into four molecular subgroups (WNT, SHH, Group 3, Group 4) with very different prognoses. Treatment combines surgery, radiation, and chemotherapy, with intensity adjusted by risk. You'll hear medulloblastoma in pediatric neuro-oncology discussions and in any rare-pediatric-cancer biotech pitch.

snappy: A class of drugs that block MEK enzymes in the MAPK signaling pathway — usually combined with BRAF inhibitors for stronger and more durable cancer control.
detail: MEK inhibitors include Mekinist (trametinib), Cotellic (cobimetinib), and Mektovi (binimetinib). They're standard partners for BRAF inhibitors in melanoma, where the combination beats single-agent BRAF and delays resistance. MEK inhibitors are also approved in NF1-related plexiform neurofibromas. You'll hear MEK inhibitors in melanoma trial readouts and rare-disease pediatric tumor discussions.

snappy: An aggressive skin cancer arising from pigment-producing cells — historically deadly when advanced, transformed by checkpoint inhibitors and BRAF-targeted therapy.
detail: About 50% of melanomas carry BRAF mutations. Treatment options include immunotherapy (Keytruda, Opdivo, Yervoy combinations) and BRAF/MEK combos like Tafinlar+Mekinist. Five-year survival in metastatic melanoma rose from <10% pre-2011 to >50% today. You'll hear melanoma at every oncology conference and as the historical reference cancer for immuno-oncology success.

snappy: A rare cancer of the lining around the lungs and abdomen — almost always linked to past asbestos exposure, often with decades between exposure and diagnosis.
detail: Mesothelioma is uncommon but devastating, with median survival of about a year despite combination treatments including surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, and increasingly immunotherapy with Opdivo plus Yervoy. The disease has a long history in legal contexts due to asbestos liability. You'll hear mesothelioma at thoracic-oncology meetings and in legal/insurance contexts.

snappy: A receptor on cells that signals for growth — when mutated or amplified, it drives a small but treatable subset of lung and other cancers.
detail: MET exon 14 skipping mutations and MET amplifications occur in 3-5% of lung cancers. Targeted drugs Tabrecta (capmatinib) and Tepmetko (tepotinib) are FDA-approved for MET exon 14-mutated NSCLC. You'll hear MET in lung-cancer molecular-testing discussions and in commercial pitches about niche-but-defensible NSCLC subtypes.

snappy: What a drug becomes after your body breaks it down — sometimes inactive, sometimes the actual active form, sometimes the source of side effects.
detail: Some drugs are administered as inactive prodrugs that get converted to active metabolites in the liver (e.g., codeine to morphine). Others have toxic metabolites that explain side effects. Drug development pays close attention to metabolite profiles. You'll hear metabolite in pharmacology discussions, in DDI conversations, and at FDA reviews of drug-drug interaction risk.

snappy: The spread of cancer from where it started to other parts of the body — and the cause of most cancer deaths, since localized tumors are usually treatable.
detail: Metastasis involves cancer cells breaking away, traveling through blood or lymph, and establishing new tumors elsewhere. The biology is complex; preventing or treating metastasis is one of oncology's hardest problems. Most stage IV cancer trials are about metastatic-disease control. You'll hear metastasis in every oncology context — clinical, research, and commercial.

snappy: A DNA-repair enzyme that fights chemotherapy — when its gene is silenced (methylated) in glioblastoma, the tumor responds much better to standard chemo.
detail: MGMT removes the DNA damage caused by temozolomide, the standard chemo for glioblastoma. Tumors with MGMT methylation can't repair this damage well, leading to better treatment response. MGMT methylation is the most established prognostic biomarker in GBM. You'll hear MGMT in neuro-oncology trial enrollment and stratification discussions.

snappy: The trillions of bacteria, fungi, and viruses living in and on your body — increasingly recognized as a factor in how well drugs work, especially immunotherapy.
detail: Studies have linked specific gut bacteria to better or worse responses to checkpoint inhibitors. The field is in early days, with fecal microbiota transplants and engineered microbial therapeutics in clinical testing. You'll hear microbiome in immuno-oncology research, in biotech pipelines from companies like Vedanta and Seres, and in commercial conversations about response prediction.

snappy: A pattern of widespread DNA repair errors — found in about 5% of cancers — that makes tumors visible to the immune system and unusually responsive to immunotherapy.
detail: MSI-H tumors arise from defective DNA mismatch repair, leading to high mutation loads that produce neoantigens. Keytruda became the first tumor-agnostic FDA approval based on MSI-H status in 2017, treating any solid tumor with this biomarker. You'll hear MSI-H in colorectal, endometrial, and tumor-agnostic oncology contexts.

snappy: A severe, often disabling headache — usually one-sided, throbbing, and accompanied by nausea, light sensitivity, or visual auras — that can knock people out for hours or days.
detail: Acute migraine treatment ranges from triptans like sumatriptan to the newer gepants — Nurtec (rimegepant), Ubrelvy (ubrogepant). Prevention has been transformed by anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies (Aimovig/erenumab, Ajovy, Emgality) and oral CGRP-targeting drugs. You'll hear migraine pitched on AbbVie, Pfizer, and Lilly earnings calls as a high-growth specialty market, and at AHS when head-to-head data on CGRP drugs reshape prescribing — it's one of the most actively prescribed-by-neurologists categories.

snappy: A treatment that delivers a strand of genetic instructions to your cells, telling them to make a protein — used in COVID vaccines and now expanding to cancer.
detail: Messenger RNA was a niche idea until Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna's COVID vaccines validated the platform at global scale. The same technology now powers personalized cancer vaccines, including Moderna and Merck's mRNA-4157 paired with Keytruda (pembrolizumab) in melanoma. You'll hear mRNA pitched as a platform play at JPMorgan and on R&D days, and trial readouts in oncology and rare disease are increasingly studied as the next proof points beyond infectious disease.

snappy: A blood cancer of plasma cells in the bone marrow — historically incurable, but now manageable for many years through repeated treatments.
detail: MM treatment has been transformed by IMiDs (Revlimid, Pomalyst), proteasome inhibitors (Velcade, Kyprolis), CD38 antibodies (Darzalex, Sarclisa), CAR-T (Carvykti, Abecma), and bispecifics (Tecvayli, Talvey). Survival has roughly doubled in 20 years. You'll hear MM at ASH and in any hematology pipeline discussion of B-cell-malignancy expansion.

snappy: An autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks the protective coating of nerves — disrupting signals between brain and body and causing symptoms that flare and remit.
detail: MS treatment is built around disease-modifying therapies that reduce relapses: high-efficacy infused drugs like Ocrevus (ocrelizumab) and Tysabri (natalizumab), oral options like Mavenclad (cladribine) and Tecfidera (dimethyl fumarate), and a growing class of S1P modulators. You'll hear MS at Roche, Biogen, and Novartis earnings calls as a fiercely competitive market, and at ECTRIMS and AAN when new readouts on remyelination or progressive MS shift competitive positioning.

snappy: A change in the genetic code — sometimes harmless, sometimes the spark that turns a normal cell into a cancer cell.
detail: Cancer-driving mutations include single-letter changes (point mutations like KRAS G12C), insertions/deletions, fusions, and chromosomal rearrangements. Modern oncology revolves around identifying them, designing drugs against them, and tracking when they evolve to cause resistance. You'll hear mutation everywhere in oncology — in trial designs, FDA labels, and pathology reports.

snappy: A 'master regulator' gene that controls cell growth — overactive in many cancers but historically considered impossible to drug directly.
detail: MYC amplification or activation is one of the most common cancer changes — found in around 70% of human cancers. Direct MYC inhibitors have been an industry holy grail; current strategies focus on indirect approaches like targeting MYC partners or downstream pathways. You'll hear MYC in any biotech pitch claiming to have cracked an undruggable target.

snappy: A cousin of the MYC gene — when amplified in neuroblastoma, it's the single biggest predictor of aggressive disease and poor outcomes in children.
detail: MYCN amplification occurs in about 20% of neuroblastomas and defines a high-risk group requiring intensive treatment. The amplification has resisted direct targeting; alternative strategies include BET inhibitors and aurora kinase inhibitors in clinical trials. You'll hear MYCN in pediatric oncology, in neuroblastoma research, and in rare-cancer biotech pitches.

snappy: A type of head and neck cancer that grows behind the nose — strongly tied to Epstein-Barr virus and disproportionately common in parts of Asia.
detail: NPC is heavily linked to EBV infection and is treated with radiation, often combined with platinum-based chemo and increasingly checkpoint inhibitors. The disease has a sharp geographic distribution — common in southern China and Southeast Asia, rare in the US. You'll hear NPC in head-and-neck oncology contexts and in any global oncology pipeline including Asian markets.

snappy: A nonprofit alliance of major US cancer centers that publishes the most-followed treatment guidelines in oncology — read by doctors, payers, and pharma.
detail: NCCN guidelines define what counts as standard-of-care for each cancer type and stage, updated continuously by expert panels. Insurers use NCCN to decide what they'll cover; pharma teams target NCCN listings to drive prescribing. You'll hear NCCN in any commercial discussion of clinical-guideline inclusion and in oncology medical-affairs strategy.

snappy: The US government's main cancer-research agency — funding science, running trials, and publishing the plain-language definitions used across oncology.
detail: NCI is part of the NIH and the world's largest funder of cancer research. Its programs include the Cancer Moonshot, the SEER cancer registry, and the NCI Dictionary of Cancer Terms (a key glossary source). You'll hear NCI in academic oncology, clinical-trials infrastructure (NCI-MATCH), and federal cancer-policy discussions.

snappy: The big package a company submits to the FDA asking for approval to sell a new drug — typically thousands of pages of clinical, manufacturing, and safety data.
detail: NDAs are filed for small-molecule drugs (BLAs are filed for biologics). Once accepted, the FDA has 10 months for standard review or 6 for priority review under PDUFA. NDA filing acceptance is itself a stock-moving event. You'll hear NDA in any biotech regulatory milestone announcement and in earnings calls projecting approval timelines.

snappy: Uncontrolled cell death — usually caused by injury, infection, or lack of blood supply — that triggers inflammation and tissue damage.
detail: Unlike apoptosis (clean, programmed cell death), necrosis is messy and inflammatory. In tumors, central necrosis often appears as growth outpaces blood supply. Pathologists describe necrosis grade in tumor reports as part of aggressiveness assessment. You'll hear necrosis in pathology, in tumor imaging discussions, and in clinical trial response assessment.

snappy: Treatment given before the main therapy — usually before surgery — to shrink a tumor or test how well a drug works while there's still tumor to measure.
detail: Neoadjuvant therapy is widely used in breast, rectal, lung, and bladder cancer, often delivering chemotherapy, hormone therapy, or immunotherapy before surgery. The pathologic complete response rate (pCR) at surgery is a powerful early efficacy signal. You'll hear neoadjuvant constantly at oncology conferences and in any drug pitch claiming early-disease impact.

snappy: The medical word for a 'new growth' in the body — a tumor that may be benign or cancerous, but in either case represents abnormal cell proliferation.
detail: Not all neoplasms are cancer; benign neoplasms (lipomas, fibroids) can grow but don't invade or spread. Malignant neoplasms are cancers. The term shows up in pathology reports and ICD codes. You'll hear neoplasm in formal medical writing, in tumor classification, and in cancer registry data.

snappy: A diverse family of cancers arising from hormone-producing cells — slow-growing in many cases, but capable of secreting hormones that cause distinctive symptoms.
detail: NETs include carcinoids, pancreatic NETs, and pheochromocytomas. Treatment depends on grade and location: surgery, somatostatin analogs (Octreotide, Sandostatin), peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (Lutathera), and targeted drugs (Afinitor, Sutent). You'll hear NET at GI and endocrine oncology meetings and in radioligand-therapy commercial discussions.

snappy: A childhood cancer that arises from immature nerve tissue — typically diagnosed before age 5 and ranging from spontaneously regressing to highly aggressive.
detail: Neuroblastoma is the most common cancer in infants. High-risk disease (often MYCN-amplified) requires intensive multimodal treatment including surgery, chemo, radiation, stem-cell transplant, and the GD2-targeting antibody Unituxin (dinutuximab). You'll hear neuroblastoma in pediatric oncology, in CAR-T expansion attempts, and in any rare-pediatric-cancer drug-development pitch.

snappy: Nerve damage that can cause numbness, tingling, or pain — a common side effect of certain chemotherapy drugs and a key dose-limiting toxicity.
detail: Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN) is associated with platinum drugs, taxanes, vincristine, and bortezomib. It can be permanent and significantly affects quality of life. Newer ADCs like Enhertu also carry neuropathy risk. You'll hear neuropathy in trial safety profiles, in patient experience discussions, and in any commercial framing of tolerability advantages.

snappy: A low count of neutrophils — a key infection-fighting white blood cell — usually caused by chemotherapy and the main reason cancer patients risk dangerous infections.
detail: Severe neutropenia (febrile neutropenia) is a medical emergency. Growth factors like Neulasta (pegfilgrastim) help reduce risk. Most chemotherapy regimens monitor and adjust dose around neutrophil counts. You'll hear neutropenia in oncology safety discussions, in supportive-care drug development, and in any commercial framing of safer chemo regimens.

snappy: A high-throughput technology that reads millions of stretches of DNA at once — making it cheap and fast to find the mutations driving a person's cancer.
detail: NGS underpins modern precision oncology. Comprehensive genomic profiling tests (Foundation Medicine, Tempus, Caris) sequence hundreds of cancer-related genes from tumor tissue or blood. You'll hear NGS in oncology pathology, in companion-diagnostic discussions, and in any commercial pitch around biomarker-driven drug selection.

snappy: The big umbrella covering all the lymphomas that aren't Hodgkin lymphoma — a wildly diverse group ranging from indolent to dramatically aggressive.
detail: NHL includes DLBCL, follicular lymphoma, mantle cell lymphoma, marginal zone lymphoma, and many rarer subtypes. Treatment depends entirely on subtype and stage; B-cell-targeted drugs (rituximab, BTK inhibitors, BCL-2 inhibitors, CAR-T, bispecifics) dominate. You'll hear NHL constantly at ASH and in any pharma pipeline discussion of B-cell malignancies.

snappy: A trial designed to prove a new drug is at least as good as the existing standard — not necessarily better, just close enough not to be worse.
detail: Non-inferiority is the design pharma reaches for when the new drug offers something other than raw efficacy — better tolerability, simpler dosing, lower cost. The trial sets a "margin" — typically how much worse the new drug could be while still being acceptable — and tries to show the difference falls inside it. You'll see this design in cardiovascular trials of new anticoagulants and infectious-disease readouts where the bar is matching, not beating, the standard.

snappy: A signaling pathway that decides cell fate during development — when mutated, it can drive certain leukemias and several solid tumors.
detail: NOTCH1 is mutated in about 60% of T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemias (T-ALL) and is implicated in some breast cancers and triple-negative subtypes. Direct NOTCH inhibitors (gamma-secretase inhibitors) have been challenging to develop due to GI toxicity. You'll hear NOTCH in pediatric leukemia research and in early-stage targeted-therapy pipelines.

snappy: The major form of lung cancer — about 85% of cases — and the part of oncology that has been most transformed by precision medicine and immunotherapy.
detail: NSCLC includes adenocarcinoma, squamous cell, and large-cell types. Treatment is dominated by molecular testing (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, MET, RET, NTRK, KRAS G12C) plus immunotherapy with Keytruda or Opdivo for non-targetable cases. You'll hear NSCLC at every oncology conference and in nearly every pharma oncology pipeline discussion.

snappy: A family of genes whose rare fusions can drive cancers across many different tissues — and which are fully treatable with two FDA-approved targeted drugs.
detail: NTRK fusions occur in less than 1% of common cancers but in nearly all of certain rare cancers (infantile fibrosarcoma, secretory breast carcinoma). Vitrakvi (larotrectinib) and Rozlytrek (entrectinib) are tumor-agnostic FDA approvals — they treat any NTRK-positive cancer regardless of organ. You'll hear NTRK in tumor-agnostic-approval discussions and as a model for biomarker-driven drug development.

snappy: A second-generation antibody against the CD20 protein on B-cells — designed to be more potent than rituximab against certain lymphomas and CLL.
detail: Gazyva is approved with chemo for CLL, follicular lymphoma, and DLBCL. It's a glycoengineered antibody intended to recruit immune cells more effectively than its predecessor. You'll hear obinutuzumab in CD20 lifecycle-management discussions, especially as rituximab biosimilars erode the original franchise.

snappy: Prescribing a drug for a condition or population the FDA hasn't formally approved it for — legal for doctors but heavily restricted for manufacturers to promote.
detail: Off-label prescribing is common in oncology, especially when guidelines outpace formal approvals. Pharma can't market off-label uses, but doctors and payers may rely on NCCN guidelines for decisions. You'll hear off-label in commercial-strategy discussions, FDA enforcement (Department of Justice settlements over off-label promotion are major events), and reimbursement debates.

snappy: The first PARP inhibitor approved by the FDA — pioneered the strategy of attacking cancers whose DNA-repair machinery is already broken.
detail: Lynparza is approved for BRCA-mutated ovarian, breast, prostate, and pancreatic cancers, exploiting synthetic lethality (the broken DNA repair plus PARP inhibition is too much for the cancer cell). It launched the PARP inhibitor class. You'll hear olaparib in any PARP-inhibitor commercial discussion, in BRCA-testing strategies, and in maintenance-therapy economics.

snappy: Cancer that has spread, but only to a small number of sites — a state where aggressive local treatment of every spot may produce long-term disease control.
detail: Oligometastatic disease (typically 1-5 metastases) is being increasingly treated with stereotactic radiosurgery or targeted surgery alongside systemic therapy, sometimes producing years of disease-free survival. The concept has reshaped how doctors think about advanced cancer. You'll hear oligometastatic at radiation-oncology meetings and in commercial discussions of localized-treatment platforms.

snappy: A gene that, when activated or amplified abnormally, drives cells toward cancer — the opposite of a tumor-suppressor gene.
detail: Classic oncogenes include MYC, RAS family, HER2, and many kinases. They're often the targets of precision-oncology drugs because activation creates dependency: cancers driven by an oncogene need it to survive, making them vulnerable to inhibitors. You'll hear oncogene in basic cancer biology and in any drug-discovery pitch around 'oncogene-addicted' tumors.

snappy: A doctor specializing in cancer treatment — usually subdivided into medical, surgical, radiation, and pediatric oncology, each handling a different piece of care.
detail: Medical oncologists prescribe chemo, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy; surgical oncologists remove tumors; radiation oncologists deliver radiation. Tumor boards bring all three together to make treatment decisions. You'll hear oncologist in patient-experience discussions, in commercial sales-targeting conversations, and in any clinical-trial-investigator context.

snappy: The medical specialty dedicated to diagnosing and treating cancer — one of the largest, fastest-growing, and most innovation-heavy areas of medicine.
detail: Oncology is divided into many subspecialties: medical, surgical, radiation, pediatric, hematologic, gynecologic, and so on. The field has been the leading edge of pharma innovation for two decades and accounts for the largest share of new drug approvals. You'll hear oncology referenced as the dominant therapeutic area in nearly every pharma strategy discussion.

snappy: A clinical trial where everyone — patients, doctors, sponsors — knows who's getting what treatment, in contrast to blinded trials.
detail: Open-label trials are common in early-phase oncology and rare diseases where placebo isn't ethical or practical. The lack of blinding can introduce bias, especially for patient-reported outcomes, so open-label results often need confirmation. You'll hear open-label in trial-design discussions and in any FDA review where bias-mitigation is debated.

snappy: A drug for a rare disease — defined in the US as fewer than 200,000 patients — that gets FDA incentives like extended exclusivity and tax credits to make development worthwhile.
detail: Orphan Drug Designation grants 7 years of market exclusivity, 25% tax credits on clinical trial costs, and waiver of FDA application fees. The Orphan Drug Act of 1983 transformed rare-disease drug development. You'll hear orphan drug in rare-disease biotech pitches, in pricing debates (orphan drugs often cost more), and in regulatory affairs strategy.

snappy: The percentage of patients in a trial whose tumors shrink by a defined amount after treatment — a fast, early measure of whether a drug is doing anything.
detail: ORR is a common endpoint for accelerated approval, especially in single-arm Phase 2 trials of targeted therapies. It's faster to measure than survival, but doesn't tell you about durability or whether patients live longer. You'll hear ORR in every oncology trial readout and in regulatory discussions about confirmatory-trial requirements.

snappy: The gold-standard cancer trial outcome: how long patients actually live from the start of treatment — the metric the FDA most wants to see improved.
detail: OS is the unambiguous endpoint — patients are either alive or not — but it takes years to mature, especially in cancers with long-term outcomes like indolent lymphomas. Trials use surrogate endpoints (PFS, ORR) to support earlier approvals, with OS as the confirmatory measure. You'll hear OS in every oncology trial readout and at FDA advisory committees evaluating accelerated approvals.

snappy: A third-generation EGFR inhibitor that became the standard first-line treatment for EGFR-mutant lung cancer — and AstraZeneca's biggest oncology product.
detail: Tagrisso targets the T790M resistance mutation that defeated earlier EGFR drugs and is now used in front-line and adjuvant EGFR-mutant NSCLC. With sales over $5B annually, it's a cornerstone of AstraZeneca's oncology franchise. You'll hear Tagrisso at lung-cancer trial readouts, AstraZeneca earnings calls, and any commercial discussion of EGFR lifecycle management.

snappy: The most common primary bone cancer — typically affecting teenagers in growing bones — and a disease where treatment hasn't meaningfully changed in decades.
detail: Osteosarcoma treatment has been combination chemotherapy plus limb-sparing surgery since the 1980s, with cure rates of about 70% in localized disease and far lower in metastatic disease. The lack of progress has made it a focus of pediatric and rare-cancer drug development. You'll hear osteosarcoma at pediatric oncology meetings and in rare-disease biotech pitches.

snappy: Drugs you can buy without a prescription — pulled off the shelf based on FDA monographs that decide which ingredients are safe for self-care.
detail: OTC drugs cover everything from ibuprofen to nicotine patches to the recently approved Opill — the first non-prescription daily birth control pill in the US. The FDA can switch a drug from prescription to OTC after evidence shows consumers can use it safely without a doctor, a process called "Rx-to-OTC switch." You'll hear it in commercial discussions when companies pitch OTC switches as a second life for aging brands losing exclusivity.

snappy: A frequently fatal gynecologic cancer that often spreads silently before being detected — and a disease where PARP inhibitors have been transformative.
detail: Most ovarian cancers are epithelial; high-grade serous (often BRCA- or HRD-driven) is the dominant subtype. Treatment is surgery plus platinum-based chemo, with PARP inhibitors (Lynparza, Zejula, Rubraca) as standard maintenance. You'll hear ovarian cancer at gynecologic oncology meetings and in any commercial discussion of PARP-inhibitor-driven franchise economics.

snappy: The 'guardian of the genome' — a tumor suppressor that triggers cell death when DNA damage is too severe. Disabled in more than half of all cancers.
detail: TP53 mutations are the most common genetic change in cancer, found in around 50% of solid tumors. When p53 fails, damaged cells survive and accumulate further mutations. Restoring p53 function or exploiting p53-deficient biology has been a long-running drug-development goal. You'll hear p53 in any cancer-mechanism discussion and in pipeline pitches around p53-pathway therapies.

snappy: A chemotherapy drug originally derived from the Pacific yew tree — works by jamming cancer cells in a mid-division state until they self-destruct.
detail: Paclitaxel and the related docetaxel are mainstays of breast, ovarian, lung, and pancreatic cancer treatment. The mechanism — stabilizing microtubules — is distinctive among chemo drugs. Generic and nanoparticle-bound (Abraxane) formulations are widely used. You'll hear paclitaxel in oncology trial designs as a comparator and in any chemo backbone discussion.

snappy: A DNA-repair gene that works alongside BRCA1 and BRCA2 — when mutated, it raises breast and pancreatic cancer risk and predicts PARP-inhibitor response.
detail: PALB2 mutations are less common than BRCA but functionally similar, and PALB2-mutant tumors often respond to PARP inhibitors. Genetic testing panels routinely include PALB2 alongside BRCA. You'll hear PALB2 in hereditary-cancer counseling and in commercial discussions of expanded biomarker eligibility for PARP-inhibitor labels.

snappy: Care focused on relieving symptoms and maximizing quality of life rather than curing disease — important at any cancer stage, not just end of life.
detail: Palliative care includes pain management, symptom control, and psychosocial support. It's increasingly delivered alongside curative treatment, not as an alternative to it. Studies show early palliative care can extend survival in some cancers. You'll hear palliative in supportive-oncology discussions, in commercial pitches around symptom-management drugs, and in healthcare policy debates.

snappy: One of the deadliest common cancers — usually diagnosed late, with median survival under a year despite recent advances in chemotherapy combinations.
detail: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) carries KRAS mutations in over 90% of cases, traditionally considered undruggable. Standard treatment is FOLFIRINOX or gemcitabine-based chemo, with KRAS G12C inhibitors and CAR-T trials emerging. You'll hear pancreatic cancer at GI oncology meetings, in 'underserved-indication' biotech pitches, and in any KRAS-targeted-therapy commercial discussion.

snappy: A progressive movement disorder caused by the loss of dopamine-producing brain cells — leading to tremor, stiffness, and slowness, and often affecting mood and thinking later.
detail: Parkinson's treatment has been dominated for 50 years by levodopa, which replaces missing dopamine but loses effectiveness over time. Newer extended-release and inhaled formulations — Inbrija (inhaled levodopa), Crexont — and adjuncts like MAO-B inhibitors fill gaps. Disease-modifying therapy remains the holy grail. You'll hear Parkinson's at every neurology investor day where companies pitch alpha-synuclein and GBA-targeted programs, and at MDS Congress when surgical approaches like deep brain stimulation get reframed by new device generations.

snappy: A class of drugs that block a key DNA-repair enzyme — devastating to tumors that already have broken DNA-repair machinery (especially BRCA-mutated cancers).
detail: PARP inhibitors (Lynparza, Zejula, Rubraca, Talzenna) work via 'synthetic lethality' — combining PARP inhibition with existing BRCA mutations is too much for cancer cells to survive. Approved across ovarian, breast, prostate, and pancreatic cancer with BRCA or HRD biomarkers. You'll hear PARP inhibitors at every breast/ovarian/prostate cancer trial readout.

snappy: When surgery after pre-treatment shows no remaining live cancer in the removed tissue — the cleanest signal that a drug is working.
detail: pCR rates are increasingly used as accelerated-approval endpoints in early-stage breast cancer, where they correlate (imperfectly) with long-term survival benefit. Drugs that meaningfully boost pCR can advance to FDA approval years before survival data matures. You'll hear pCR in neoadjuvant breast-cancer trials and in commercial pitches around early-disease drug positioning.

snappy: The middleman between drugmakers, insurers, and pharmacies — they negotiate prices, decide what's covered, and ultimately shape what you pay at the counter.
detail: The big three — CVS Caremark, Express Scripts, and OptumRx — manage formularies for most US insurance plans, extracting rebates from pharma in exchange for preferred placement. They've been a frequent target of Congressional hearings and FTC scrutiny over how rebates flow. You'll hear PBM constantly in pharma earnings calls when companies discuss pricing pressure, and at JPMorgan when launch teams game out access strategy.

snappy: The immune checkpoint that cancer most often hijacks to hide — and the target of the biggest immunotherapy success story in oncology.
detail: PD-1 sits on T-cells; PD-L1 sits on cancer cells. When they bind, T-cells are switched off. Drugs blocking either side — Keytruda, Opdivo, Tecentriq, Imfinzi, Bavencio — have transformed melanoma, lung, kidney, bladder, head-and-neck, and many other cancers. You'll hear PD-1/PD-L1 at every immuno-oncology discussion and in essentially every solid-tumor commercial conversation.

snappy: The deal where drug companies pay the FDA to review their applications on a clock — and Congress renews it every five years.
detail: PDUFA sets the FDA's review timeline — typically 10 months for standard drugs, 6 months for priority ones — and is funded by industry fees rather than taxpayers. Investors and journalists watch the "PDUFA date" the way they watch earnings: it's the day the FDA must rule on whether a drug gets approved.

snappy: Merck's checkpoint inhibitor — the best-selling cancer drug in the world, and the first ever approved based on a biomarker rather than a tumor type.
detail: Keytruda blocks PD-1, with approvals across more than 20 cancer types. The 2017 MSI-H tumor-agnostic approval was a regulatory landmark. With sales above $25B annually, Keytruda dominates immuno-oncology economics. You'll hear Keytruda at every oncology conference, in any combination-therapy pipeline pitch, and in patent-cliff discussions about Merck's late-2020s exposure.

snappy: A drug made of a short chain of amino acids — bigger than a small molecule, smaller than a full antibody — designed to hit targets neither extreme can reach.
detail: Peptide drugs sit between traditional pills and biologics, and the GLP-1 class — Ozempic (semaglutide), Mounjaro (tirzepatide), Wegovy — has made them the most-watched drug category in pharma. Other approved peptides include Sandostatin (octreotide) for hormone-secreting tumors and oral GLP-1s now reaching late-stage trials. You'll hear "peptide" everywhere now in earnings calls about obesity and diabetes pipelines, and at JPMorgan when companies pitch their position in the GLP-1 race.

snappy: Treatment chosen based on the specific genetic and molecular features of each patient's disease — instead of a one-size-fits-all approach.
detail: Precision medicine in oncology means testing tumors for specific mutations and matching patients to drugs targeting those mutations. The approach has been transformative in lung cancer, melanoma, and several blood cancers. You'll hear personalized medicine in any trial-design discussion involving biomarker selection, in companion-diagnostic strategy, and in any commercial pitch about niche-but-defensible markets.

snappy: The time from treatment start until the disease gets worse — a faster-to-measure efficacy endpoint than overall survival, widely used in oncology trials.
detail: PFS captures both stable disease and tumor shrinkage, ending when imaging or symptoms show clear progression. It's the most common primary endpoint in metastatic-cancer trials and supports many accelerated approvals. You'll hear PFS in every oncology trial readout, in FDA review discussions, and in commercial pitches around treatment durability.

snappy: The ongoing job of watching for safety problems after a drug is on the market — collecting reports, spotting patterns, and acting before a small signal becomes a crisis.
detail: Pharmacovigilance — "PV" in industry shorthand — combines spontaneous adverse-event reports (FDA's FAERS, EMA's EudraVigilance), claims data, and active surveillance to catch risks trials missed. PV findings can trigger label changes, Black Box Warnings, or full withdrawals — Vioxx (rofecoxib) is the textbook example. You'll see PV teams cited in every FDA safety communication, and in earnings calls when a competitor's safety signal becomes a market opportunity.

snappy: The standard sequence of human drug testing — Phase 1 finds the safe dose, Phase 2 tests effectiveness, Phase 3 confirms benefit in large populations.
detail: Phase 1 enrolls small groups (often healthy volunteers, or patients in oncology) for safety. Phase 2 tests efficacy in target populations. Phase 3 is the pivotal large randomized trial that supports FDA approval. Phase 4 happens post-approval. You'll hear phase numbers everywhere in pharma — they tell you the stakes and the data quality at a glance.

snappy: A specific chromosomal swap that creates the BCR-ABL fusion gene — found in chronic myeloid leukemia and a subset of acute lymphoblastic leukemia.
detail: The Philadelphia chromosome (translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22) was the first genetic abnormality consistently linked to a specific cancer. Its product, BCR-ABL, is the target of imatinib and successor TKIs. Ph+ ALL has worse prognosis than Ph-negative ALL but is now treatable with TKI-chemo combinations. You'll hear Philadelphia chromosome in CML and ALL discussions and as a historical landmark in cancer genetics.

snappy: A central signaling pathway that pushes cells to grow and survive — frequently mutated in cancer, and a perennial drug-development target with mixed results.
detail: PIK3CA mutations occur in around 40% of breast cancers and many other tumors. Approved PI3K inhibitors include Piqray (alpelisib) for breast cancer and several drugs for B-cell lymphomas, though several drugs in this class have been withdrawn due to safety. You'll hear PI3K in breast cancer trial readouts, in pathway-targeted commercial pitches, and in safety-signal discussions.

snappy: Pharmacokinetics is what your body does to the drug; pharmacodynamics is what the drug does to your body — together they predict how a treatment will behave.
detail: PK/PD modeling supports dose selection, drug-drug interaction predictions, and special-population dosing (renal impairment, pediatric). It's central to every drug-development program from Phase 1 onward. You'll hear PK/PD in any clinical pharmacology discussion, in FDA review divisions of clinical pharmacology, and in dose-optimization debates.

snappy: An inactive substance — usually a sugar pill or saline injection — given to a control group in trials to isolate the real effect of the drug being tested.
detail: Placebos can produce real measurable improvements (the placebo effect), so trials use them to establish whether a drug's benefit is from the drug itself. Placebo controls aren't ethical when standard-of-care exists for serious conditions, so trials often compare to active comparators instead. You'll hear placebo in every randomized controlled trial discussion.

snappy: A class of chemotherapy drugs (cisplatin, carboplatin, oxaliplatin) that kill cancer cells by binding to and damaging their DNA.
detail: Platinum-based chemo is a backbone for lung, ovarian, bladder, head-and-neck, and germ-cell cancers. Side effects include kidney damage, nerve damage, and hearing loss, which limit dosing. Platinum-resistance is a major treatment problem. You'll hear platinum in any chemo-comparator trial design and in commercial discussions of post-platinum treatment lines.

snappy: All the laboratory and animal testing a drug must complete before it can be given to humans — typically years of work and millions of dollars.
detail: Preclinical research includes target validation, in vitro screening, animal efficacy and toxicology, ADME profiling, and CMC development. Most drug candidates fail at this stage. You'll hear preclinical in early-stage biotech pipeline discussions, at investor briefings on near-term IND filings, and in academic licensing conversations.

snappy: A daily pill or injection that lowers a person's risk of getting HIV by roughly 99% when taken as directed — a public-health breakthrough hiding in plain sight.
detail: PrEP is delivered as oral Truvada (emtricitabine-tenofovir) and Descovy, plus the long-acting injectable Apretude (cabotegravir) given every two months. Apretude addresses adherence, the biggest weak point of daily pills. Yet uptake remains far below need, especially in Southern US states most affected by HIV. You'll hear PrEP at Gilead and ViiV earnings calls, in CDC End the HIV Epidemic updates, and in policy debates about Medicaid and 340B coverage that determine whether PrEP actually reaches at-risk populations.

snappy: The single, pre-declared measurement a clinical trial is built to answer — the outcome the whole study is powered to detect, win or lose.
detail: Companies pick a primary endpoint before the trial starts: overall survival, progression-free survival, A1C reduction, blood-pressure drop. Hitting it is the difference between a positive readout and a failed one — and statistical adjustments make it harder to claim wins on backup measures if the primary misses. You'll hear "hit the primary" celebrated on the day of trial readouts, and "missed the primary" wipe billions off market caps within minutes.

snappy: The original cancer site where the disease started — distinct from metastases, which are where the cancer has since spread.
detail: The primary tumor's tissue type usually defines the cancer (e.g., a breast tumor that has spread to the lungs is still called breast cancer). Treatment is generally based on primary-tumor type, though tumor-agnostic biomarker-driven drugs (NTRK, MSI-H) are growing in importance. You'll hear primary tumor in pathology, in cancer staging, and in commercial market-segmentation discussions.

snappy: The paperwork hurdle where your doctor has to convince your insurer that a specific drug is medically necessary before they'll cover it.
detail: Prior auth — "PA" in industry shorthand — is the most common utilization-management tool insurers use to gate expensive drugs, often requiring documentation of failed alternatives or specific diagnostic criteria. Physicians complain it consumes hours of staff time per case and delays care. You'll see it cited as a launch barrier on pharma earnings calls, and it's a frequent target in policy reform conversations from both parties in Congress.

snappy: The most common cancer in men — often slow-growing and detected through PSA screening — with a wide spectrum from watch-and-wait to aggressive lethal disease.
detail: Treatment depends on stage and risk: active surveillance for low-risk disease, surgery or radiation for localized disease, hormone therapy and increasingly androgen-receptor inhibitors (Xtandi, Erleada, Nubeqa) plus PARP inhibitors and PSMA-targeted radioligand therapy for advanced cases. You'll hear prostate cancer at GU oncology meetings and in commercial discussions of androgen-axis franchises.

snappy: A new class of drug molecules that grab a disease-causing protein and a cellular waste-disposal protein at the same time — forcing the cell to destroy the bad one.
detail: PROTACs are 'event-driven' rather than 'occupancy-driven' — they don't need to fully block a protein, just trigger its degradation. Companies like Arvinas, Kymera, and C4 Therapeutics are pioneering the class. You'll hear PROTAC in next-generation small-molecule pipeline discussions and in any pitch about previously undruggable targets.

snappy: The detailed playbook for a clinical trial — covering eligibility, dosing, schedule, endpoints, safety monitoring, and statistical analysis. The trial's contract with regulators.
detail: Protocols are negotiated with FDA and IRBs before enrollment begins. Amendments require formal approval. Operationally, the protocol is what every site, investigator, and CRO follows. You'll hear protocol in any clinical-operations role, in regulatory-submissions, and in trial-design discussions where protocol design affects feasibility and cost.

snappy: A chronic autoimmune skin disease where overactive immune signals make skin cells multiply too fast — leaving thick, scaly red patches that can crack and bleed.
detail: Psoriasis treatment has moved from topical creams to a procession of biologics — TNF inhibitors, IL-17 blockers like Cosentyx (secukinumab) and Taltz (ixekizumab), and IL-23 blockers like Skyrizi (risankizumab) — each cycle delivering higher skin-clearance rates. Up to 30% of patients also develop psoriatic arthritis. You'll hear psoriasis on AbbVie, Novartis, and Lilly earnings calls as one of the most competitive biologic markets, and at AAD when new clearance data resets the standard of care.

snappy: A tumor-suppressor gene that brakes the PI3K growth pathway — when lost or inactivated, cells grow unchecked. Common in many cancers including prostate and brain.
detail: PTEN loss occurs in roughly half of advanced prostate cancers and a meaningful fraction of glioblastoma, endometrial cancer, and others. PTEN-deficient tumors are vulnerable to PI3K inhibitors and AKT inhibitors. You'll hear PTEN in precision-oncology trial-enrollment criteria and in pathway-targeted-therapy pipelines.

snappy: The testing every batch of drug must pass before being released — making sure the actual product meets every spec for purity, strength, and consistency.
detail: QC is part of GMP, run by manufacturing-facility teams. Failures lead to lot rejections, lost revenue, and sometimes drug shortages. You'll hear QC in manufacturing operations, in supply-chain discussions, and at FDA inspections where QC documentation is key audit material.

snappy: How a treatment affects a patient's day-to-day wellbeing — increasingly measured in trials, not just survival or tumor shrinkage.
detail: QoL endpoints are captured through patient-reported outcomes (PROs) covering pain, fatigue, function, and psychological wellbeing. They're particularly important for chronic-disease and palliative-setting trials. You'll hear QoL in trial-design discussions, in FDA labels (where some claims are added), and in HTA reviews where payers care about more than just efficacy.

snappy: A measure of heart rhythm — when prolonged, it warns that a drug could trigger a dangerous arrhythmia. A standard safety check in drug development.
detail: Many drugs (some kinase inhibitors, antibiotics, antipsychotics) prolong the QTc and require ECG monitoring. The FDA reviews QTc data closely; significant prolongation can lead to label restrictions or rejection. You'll hear QTc in cardiac-safety discussions, in early-clinical trial design, and in any drug-development pitch involving QT-active classes.

snappy: A breast-conserving surgery that removes about a quarter of the breast — used to take out a tumor along with a generous margin of surrounding tissue.
detail: Quadrantectomy is more extensive than a lumpectomy but less than a mastectomy, with comparable survival outcomes when combined with radiation in appropriate patients. It's most often discussed in early-stage breast cancer treatment planning. You'll hear quadrantectomy in surgical-oncology and breast-cancer treatment discussions.

snappy: A laboratory technique that measures exactly how much of a specific DNA or RNA sequence is in a sample — used to detect mutations and monitor disease.
detail: qPCR is foundational in molecular diagnostics — used for COVID testing, BCR-ABL monitoring in CML, and minimal residual disease detection. Faster and cheaper than NGS for known targets, less comprehensive. You'll hear qPCR in molecular-diagnostics labs, in MRD-monitoring strategies, and in any commercial pitch around fast turnaround molecular testing.

snappy: Non-dividing cells — cancer's hideout — that survive most chemotherapy drugs because chemo targets actively dividing cells.
detail: Quiescent (G0) cancer stem cells are thought to be a key reason cancers come back after seemingly successful treatment. Strategies to kill quiescent cells (or force them out of dormancy) are an active research area. You'll hear quiescent in cancer-stem-cell biology and in any drug-discovery pitch around dormancy-targeting therapies.

snappy: Using high-energy beams (X-rays, protons, electrons) to damage cancer cell DNA and trigger their death — usually delivered over weeks of daily treatments.
detail: RT is used in roughly half of all cancer patients, either curatively (early-stage disease) or palliatively (symptom relief). Modern techniques (IMRT, SBRT, proton therapy) allow precise targeting that spares healthy tissue. You'll hear RT at radiation-oncology meetings (ASTRO), in combined-modality treatment discussions, and in commercial debates over proton-therapy economics.

snappy: A family of signaling proteins (including BRAF and CRAF) that pass growth signals down the MAPK pathway — frequently mutated in melanoma and other cancers.
detail: BRAF is the most clinically important RAF, with V600E mutations in around 50% of melanomas, and approved drugs Tafinlar (dabrafenib), Zelboraf (vemurafenib), and Braftovi (encorafenib). Pan-RAF inhibitors targeting multiple family members are in development. You'll hear RAF in melanoma and colorectal cancer pipelines and in pathway-resistance discussions.

snappy: The RAS family of genes (KRAS, NRAS, HRAS) — among the most-mutated drivers of human cancer, controlling cell growth signaling at the cellular membrane.
detail: RAS mutations occur in roughly 30% of all cancers, with KRAS the most clinically relevant in pancreatic, colorectal, and lung. After decades of being undruggable, KRAS G12C inhibitors (Lumakras, Krazati) opened the field; pan-KRAS and KRAS G12D inhibitors are in clinical testing. You'll hear RAS in any precision-oncology discussion of newly targetable drivers.

snappy: A tumor-suppressor protein that controls when cells divide — loss of Rb function unleashes the cell cycle and is foundational to many cancers.
detail: RB1 mutations cause retinoblastoma in children; loss of Rb function is also widespread in lung, breast, prostate, and other adult cancers. Rb is the gatekeeper that CDK4/6 inhibitors work through — those drugs only help when Rb is intact. You'll hear Rb in cell-cycle pathway discussions and in CDK4/6-inhibitor trial design.

snappy: Data on how drugs perform in routine clinical practice — drawn from electronic health records, insurance claims, and patient registries — rather than controlled trials.
detail: RWE is increasingly accepted by FDA for label expansions, post-marketing safety, and rare-disease evidence generation. Companies like Flatiron, Aetion, and Komodo Health build platforms around it. You'll hear RWE in commercial conversations about market access, in payer negotiations, and in regulatory submissions for hard-to-trial populations.

snappy: A discount drugmakers pay back to insurers and PBMs after the fact — in exchange for putting their drug on the formulary.
detail: Rebates are negotiated privately between manufacturers and PBMs, with the size often tied to formulary placement and market share. They're the engine behind US drug pricing's gross-to-net gap and a major target of policy reform — the Trump and Biden administrations both proposed rebate-reform rules. You'll hear pharma CFOs reference "rebate pressure" as the biggest variable in net pricing, and analysts model rebate trajectories at JPMorgan and on launch calls.

snappy: A protein on cell surfaces or inside cells that receives biological signals — and the most common type of drug target in pharma.
detail: Receptors include G-protein-coupled receptors (the largest drug-target family), nuclear hormone receptors, kinase receptors, and many others. Drugs work as agonists (activate) or antagonists (block) at receptors. You'll hear receptor in basic pharmacology, in target-discovery pipelines, and in any drug-mechanism discussion.

snappy: When cancer comes back after treatment — either at the original site or somewhere new — the central problem oncology is trying to solve.
detail: Recurrence can be local (same site), regional (nearby lymph nodes), or distant (metastatic). The risk depends on stage at diagnosis, biology, and treatment received. Adjuvant and maintenance therapies aim to lower recurrence risk. You'll hear recurrence in every oncology survivorship discussion, in trial endpoints (disease-free survival), and in commercial pitches around early-stage drug positioning.

snappy: Disease that fails to respond to treatment — different from relapsed disease, which initially responded and then came back.
detail: Refractory cancers are typically the toughest to treat, often having molecular features that confer resistance from the start. Trials in 'relapsed/refractory' (R/R) settings are common pathways for novel mechanisms to gain initial approval before moving earlier in treatment lines. You'll hear refractory in CAR-T trial enrollment, in salvage-therapy discussions, and in drug-development sequence framing.

snappy: Everything related to the FDA, EMA, and other agencies that decide whether and how a drug can be sold — and the function within pharma that manages those interactions.
detail: Regulatory affairs covers IND filings, NDA/BLA submissions, label negotiations, and post-marketing commitments. Strong regulatory strategy can shave months off approval timelines. You'll hear regulatory in any drug-development discussion, in earnings calls about expected approvals, and in M&A diligence around assets close to approval decisions.

snappy: A state where cancer signs and symptoms are reduced or absent — partial when some disease remains, complete when no detectable disease is left.
detail: Complete remission doesn't always mean cure — microscopic disease can persist. Minimal residual disease (MRD) testing can detect what eyes and scans miss, increasingly guiding treatment decisions. You'll hear remission in hematology-oncology, in CAR-T efficacy discussions, and in commercial pitches around durable response.

snappy: A safety program the FDA can require for drugs whose benefits only outweigh the risks if specific guardrails — like patient registries or pharmacist training — stay in place.
detail: REMS programs vary in intensity: a simple medication guide for one drug, a full restricted-distribution system requiring physician certification for another. Examples include Clozaril (clozapine) for schizophrenia, which requires regular blood monitoring, and isotretinoin for acne, which requires the iPLEDGE registry to prevent fetal exposure. You'll see REMS discussed in FDA approval announcements and in commercial planning, since strict programs can substantially limit how easily a drug gets prescribed.

snappy: When a previously effective treatment stops working — typically because cancer cells evolved new mutations to escape the drug's mechanism.
detail: Resistance is the central challenge of targeted-therapy oncology. Mechanisms include new mutations (T790M in EGFR), pathway redundancy (MEK inhibition activating other pathways), and tumor heterogeneity. Combination strategies and next-generation drugs aim to delay or overcome resistance. You'll hear resistance in every targeted-therapy pipeline discussion.

snappy: A receptor that, when mutated or fused with other genes, drives certain thyroid and lung cancers — and that has approved targeted therapies.
detail: RET fusions occur in 1-2% of NSCLC and a fraction of papillary thyroid cancers; RET point mutations cause familial medullary thyroid cancer. Retevmo (selpercatinib) and Gavreto (pralsetinib) are highly active selective RET inhibitors. You'll hear RET in lung-cancer molecular-testing discussions and in commercial pitches around niche-but-defensible NSCLC subtypes.

snappy: A rare childhood eye cancer — almost always diagnosed before age 5 — caused by mutations in the RB1 tumor-suppressor gene.
detail: Retinoblastoma is rare (300-400 US cases annually) but historically devastating. Modern treatment (chemotherapy, focal therapies, occasionally enucleation) has cure rates above 95% in developed countries. The disease was the first to demonstrate the 'two-hit' tumor-suppressor model. You'll hear retinoblastoma in pediatric ophthalmology contexts and in cancer-genetics teaching.

snappy: An autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks the lining of joints — causing pain, swelling, and over time the kind of damage that deforms hands and feet.
detail: RA treatment has evolved from old-school methotrexate to TNF inhibitors like Humira (adalimumab), JAK inhibitors like Rinvoq (upadacitinib), and IL-6 blockers like Actemra (tocilizumab). Patients are typically managed by rheumatologists who escalate therapy as the disease progresses. You'll hear RA mentioned at every immunology investor day as the foundational autoimmune market, and on AbbVie and Lilly earnings calls when JAK inhibitors come up — they carry FDA Black Box Warnings for cardiovascular and cancer risk.

snappy: The molecular messenger that carries genetic instructions from DNA to make proteins — and the basis for a new wave of vaccines and therapies.
detail: RNA-targeted therapies include antisense oligonucleotides (Spinraza, Kynamro), siRNA (Onpattro, Givlaari, Leqvio), and mRNA technologies that powered COVID-19 vaccines. Companies like Alnylam, Ionis, Moderna, and BioNTech built businesses here. You'll hear RNA in vaccine pipelines, rare-disease drug development, and any biotech pitch around nucleic-acid platforms.

snappy: A natural cellular silencer that pharma has hijacked to switch off harmful genes — turning down the volume on disease at the source rather than blocking it downstream.
detail: RNAi drugs like Onpattro (patisiran) for hereditary amyloidosis and Leqvio (inclisiran) for high cholesterol use small RNA molecules to degrade specific mRNAs before they're translated into proteins. Alnylam pioneered the platform after the 2006 Nobel-winning discovery, and the field has expanded into liver, kidney, and CNS targets. You'll hear RNAi pitched as a platform alongside mRNA and gene therapy at JPMorgan, and earnings calls increasingly track the silencing-drug pipeline.

snappy: A receptor tyrosine kinase whose rare gene fusions drive a small subset of lung and other cancers — and that's strongly targetable with FDA-approved drugs.
detail: ROS1 fusions occur in 1-2% of NSCLC and are also seen in glioma, cholangiocarcinoma, and other cancers. Approved ROS1 inhibitors include Xalkori (crizotinib), Rozlytrek (entrectinib), Lorbrena (lorlatinib), and Augtyro (repotrectinib). You'll hear ROS1 in lung-cancer molecular-testing and in commercial discussions of next-generation TKIs.
